Her friends report that she became flushed and started gasping for air before losing consciousness. On arrival, her temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure is 68/42 mm Hg, pulse is 128/min, and respirations are 28/min with audible wheezing. Physical examination reveals diffuse urticaria, periorbital swelling, and use of accessory respiratory muscles. Intramuscular medication is administered immediately, followed by intravenous fluids and supplemental oxygen. Within minutes, her airway obstruction and hypotension improve significantly. The administered drug exerts its life-saving effect primarily through which of the following mechanisms?

options.B: Inhibits histamine release from mast cells
options.E: Vasodilates bronchiolar smooth muscle via nitric oxide

explanation:
Epinephrine is the cornerstone of anaphylaxis management because it directly addresses all the major life-threatening components of the reaction—airway obstruction, bronchospasm, and vascular collapse. It acts by stimulating both α- and β-adrenoreceptors, which produces complementary physiologic effects that rapidly reverse the pathophysiologic cascade triggered by massive mast cell and basophil degranulation.
Activation of α₁-adrenoreceptors causes potent vasoconstriction, increasing systemic vascular resistance and blood pressure while simultaneously reducing vascular permeability in postcapillary venules. This minimizes plasma leakage into tissues, reversing mucosal edema and preventing laryngeal obstruction. α₁ activity also reduces cutaneous flushing and swelling, which are mediated by histamine-induced vasodilation.
β₁-adrenoreceptor stimulation increases myocardial contractility and heart rate, augmenting cardiac output and improving perfusion during anaphylactic shock. At the same time, β₂-adrenoreceptor activation produces bronchodilation through smooth muscle relaxation in the airways, thereby relieving bronchospasm and wheezing. β₂ signaling also increases intracellular cyclic AMP in mast cells and basophils, which inhibits
further degranulation and release of inflammatory mediators (histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins). This secondary effect prevents the progression or recurrence of the reaction even after the initial episode is controlled.
The combined α and β stimulation explains why epinephrine is uniquely effective compared with other drugs used in allergic reactions. No other single agent can both constrict dilated vessels and relax constricted bronchioles simultaneously while also suppressing mediator release. Administration by intramuscular injection into the anterolateral thigh (vastus lateralis) achieves rapid absorption due to high vascularity, providing a
faster and safer onset than subcutaneous routes. In refractory cases, intravenous epinephrine may be used with careful monitoring due to risk of arrhythmia and severe hypertension.
Mechanistically, anaphylaxis results from widespread IgE-mediated mast cell and basophil activation following allergen exposure. Cross-linking of allergen-specific IgE on cell-surface FcεRI receptors triggers exocytosis of preformed mediators such as histamine and tryptase, along with synthesis of new mediators including leukotrienes (LTC₄, LTD₄, LTE₄) and prostaglandin D₂. These substances cause bronchoconstriction,
vasodilation, vascular permeability, and tissue edema, leading to airway narrowing and distributive shock. Epinephrine interrupts this cycle by binding adrenergic receptors, stabilizing mast cells, and counteracting both the vascular and respiratory effects.
bottom_line: Epinephrine treats anaphylaxis by activating α- and β-adrenoreceptors—raising blood pressure, relieving bronchospasm, and preventing further mediator release. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 54-year-old man presents with progressive shortness of breath and dry cough over 2 weeks. Medical history is significant for seropositive rheumatoid arthritis, seasonal allergies and newly diagnosed hypertension. He has been taking methotrexate and low-dose prednisone for the past year, and was started a month ago on propranolol and hydrochlorothiazide. He does not smoke and has no history of asthma or recent illness. Physical examination reveals end-inspiratory crackles at the lung bases. Chest x-ray reveals bilateral reticular opacities. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism responsible for this patient's pulmonary findings?

options.A: Alveolar damage due to type III hypersensitivity
options.C: Immune complex deposition in pulmonary vasculature
options.D: Inhibition of surfactant production by pneumocytes

explanation:
Methotrexate is a folate antagonist that inhibits dihydrofolate reductase, blocking DNA synthesis by preventing the formation of tetrahydrofolate. At low doses, it acts as an immunosuppressant, reducing inflammation in conditions like rheumatoid arthritis and psoriasis. At higher doses, it is used as a chemotherapeutic agent in malignancies such as leukemia, lymphoma, and osteosarcoma. Adverse effects include methotexateinduced lung injury, hepatotoxicity, bone marrow suppression, oral ulcers, alopecia, and teratogenicity. Moreover, as it is a folate antagonist, it requires supplementation with leucovorin (active form of folate).
Methotrexate-induced lung injury typically presents as subacute dyspnea, dry cough, restrictive pattern on spirometry and reticular opacities on imaging. The pathogenesis is thought to be immune-mediated, most consistent with interstitial lung disease. Methotrexate-induced pulmonary fibrosis is a non-dose-dependent adverse effect that can occur within weeks to years of therapy initiation. Histologically, it shows interstitial
lymphocytic infiltrates, non-necrotizing granulomas, and sometimes eosinophils. This adverse effect requires prompt discontinuation of the drug.
In general, when a USMLE question dedicates a significant portion of the vignette on medications, or multiple medications are mentioned, you should keep an open eye for drug adverse effects or drug-drug interactions. Other HY drugs associated with pulmonary fibrosis include amiodarone, bleomycin, cyclophosphamide, and nitrofurantoin.
bottom_line: Methotrexate is a dihydrofolate reductase inhibitors used as the first-line DMARD in RA. It causes pulmonary fibrosis, hepatotoxicity, and bone marrow suppression (neutropenia) with mouth ulcers. Reticulonodular/reticular changes on chest x-ray/CT mean honeycombing and are buzzy for pulmonary fibrosis. Leucovorin rescue (folinic acid) can be used to mitigate methotrexate toxicity. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 68-year-old woman comes to clinic due to progressive knee pain over the past year. She describes the pain as a dull ache that worsens with walking or prolonged standing and improves with rest. She has no history of trauma or joint swelling. Her medical history includes hypertension and gastroesophageal reflux disease. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her blood pressure today is 134/78 mmHg, and BMI is 31 kg/m². Physical examination reveals crepitus and decreased range of motion in both knees without effusion or warmth. X-rays show joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation in both knees. She is started on pharmacologic therapy for symptom relief. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of the drug used in this patient?
explanation:
This patient has osteoarthritis, characterized by chronic non-inflammatory joint degeneration with pain worsening on activity and joint space narrowing with osteophyte formation. The first-line pharmacologic treatment for osteoarthritis is acetaminophen, after weight loss as the primary non-pharmacologic intervention. Acetaminophen is preferred over NSAIDs because osteoarthritis is non-inflammatory, and NSAIDs confer
unnecessary renal and gastrointestinal risks without additional analgesic benefit.
Central-acting COX inhibitor describes acetaminophen’s mechanism. Although it reduces prostaglandin synthesis, it primarily acts in the central nervous system rather than peripherally. Acetaminophen decreases pain and fever but lacks significant anti-inflammatory effects in peripheral tissues, distinguishing it from NSAIDs. For this reason, it is not considered a true NSAID pharmacologically.
Acetaminophen is first-line for osteoarthritis pain because it provides analgesia without the nephrotoxicity and gastrointestinal bleeding risk associated with NSAIDs, particularly in elderly patients with comorbidities. Patients with chronic pain who take NSAIDs frequently are at risk of nephropathy and peptic ulcer disease due to systemic COX inhibition and prostaglandin suppression. NSAIDs are reserved for patients with
inadequate response to acetaminophen or additional inflammatory symptoms.
A major toxicity of acetaminophen is hepatotoxicity from its metabolite NAPQI, which accumulates in overdose. Activated charcoal can be administered immediately after ingestion to limit absorption. N-acetylcysteine is the specific antidote because it regenerates reduced glutathione, which neutralizes NAPQI and prevents hepatic necrosis.
bottom_line: Acetaminophen is the first-line pharmacologic treatment for osteoarthritis because it reduces pain via central COX inhibition without peripheral anti-inflammatory effects. Its safety profile is superior to NSAIDs in non-inflammatory conditions like OA, though overdose can cause hepatotoxicity. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 67-year-old man presents to the clinic for evaluation of chronic lower urinary tract symptoms. He reports a gradually weakening stream, occasional nocturnal urination, and a feeling that he doesn’t fully empty his bladder. He denies pain with urination, blood in the urine, or any recent infections. His medical history includes well-controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. He does not use tobacco or alcohol. Physical examination reveals normal vital signs and a soft, non-distended abdomen. A digital rectal exam shows a symmetrically enlarged, smooth, nontender prostate. Urinalysis is normal, and serum creatinine is stable. PSA is mildly elevated but ageappropriate. The patient is started on a medication that addresses the underlying hormonal pathophysiology of his condition and can reduce prostate volume over time. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of this medication?
explanation:
Benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) results from androgen-driven proliferation of periurethral stromal and epithelial cells in the transitional zone of the prostate. The principal androgen responsible is dihydrotestosterone (DHT), which is synthesized locally in prostate tissue by the conversion of circulating testosterone via the enzyme 5-alpha reductase. DHT has a significantly higher affinity for androgen receptors than
testosterone and is essential for growth and maintenance of the prostate throughout adulthood. During embryogenesis, DHT is also critical for male external genitalia development (penis, scrotum, prostate), while testosterone supports internal reproductive structures such as the vas deferens and seminal vesicles. This dual role explains the importance of both hormones across developmental stages and in adult physiology.
Patients with BPH typically present with lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS), including urinary hesitancy, weak stream, nocturia, and incomplete emptying. These symptoms are due to both static (gland enlargement) and dynamic (smooth muscle tone) components of bladder outlet obstruction. On physical exam, a symmetrically enlarged, smooth prostate without nodules is typical. Diagnosis is clinical but often supported with
urinalysis to rule out infection or hematuria, serum creatinine to assess renal function, and prostate-specific antigen (PSA) to assess prostate size and screen for malignancy. Ultrasound or post-void residual testing may be used if obstruction is suspected. Management begins with alpha-blockers for rapid symptom relief; however, 5-alpha reductase inhibitors like finasteride are used in men with larger prostates or who are at risk
for progression, as they reduce prostate volume over time.
Finasteride inhibits type II 5-alpha reductase, the isoenzyme found predominantly in the prostate, leading to decreased DHT production. This reduces androgenic stimulation of prostate tissue, resulting in gradual shrinkage and symptom improvement. Dutasteride, which inhibits both type I and II isoforms, has similar but broader inhibition. It may take 6–12 months to observe significant effects. These drugs lower PSA levels by
about 50%, and clinicians must double the measured PSA to accurately interpret cancer risk in patients on therapy. Side effects include erectile dysfunction, decreased libido, and gynecomastia in some men. Beyond its use in BPH, finasteride is also approved for androgenic alopecia (male pattern baldness), where it reduces scalp DHT levels and slows follicular miniaturization that contributes to hair loss.
The central role of DHT in external genital development is further illustrated by the rare genetic condition 5-alpha reductase deficiency, particularly of the type II isoform. This autosomal recessive disorder affects individuals with a 46,XY karyotype, who present at birth with ambiguous or female-appearing external genitalia due to insufficient DHT-mediated masculinization in utero. Despite this, testosterone levels and internal
male reproductive structures (e.g., testes, vas deferens) remain normal, as testosterone function is preserved. At puberty, rising testosterone levels can partially compensate for the enzyme deficiency, often resulting in virilization, including deepening of the voice, increased muscle mass, acne, and phallic enlargement from the clitoral-like structure. This developmental pathophysiology mirrors the adult therapeutic strategy in
BPH, where modulation of DHT—not testosterone—drives changes in external structures and tissue response, reinforcing the tissue-specific, high-potency role of DHT in androgen action.
bottom_line: Finasteride reduces the size of the prostate by inhibiting 5-alpha reductase, the enzyme responsible for converting testosterone to DHT, the main androgen driving prostatic hyperplasia. This hormonal mechanism addresses the root cause of BPH and is used for long-term reduction of prostate volume and symptom control. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 68-year-old woman comes to the clinic for evaluation of progressively worsening urinary symptoms over the past 6 months. She reports sudden urges to urinate that are difficult to suppress, followed by involuntary leakage before she can reach the bathroom. These episodes now occur multiple times daily, and she has begun limiting her fluid intake and avoiding social events out of fear of accidents. She denies dysuria, hematuria, or stress-induced leakage with coughing or sneezing. Her medical history includes hypertension and osteoarthritis, for which she takes lisinopril and acetaminophen. She does not smoke, and her last pelvic exam and urinalysis six months ago were normal. She underwent menopause at age 52 and has never used hormone replacement therapy. On examination, her vitals are normal. Abdominal exam shows no distention or tenderness. Pelvic exam reveals normal external genitalia, well-estrogenized vaginal mucosa, and no prolapse. Post-void residual volume by bladder scan is 20 mL. Urinalysis today is unremarkable. Neurologic exam is normal, with intact reflexes and no sensory deficits. The patient expresses strong interest in a medication to improve her symptoms and quality of life. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.E: Timed voiding and fluid restriction only

explanation:
This patient has urge incontinence, also known as overactive bladder (OAB), characterized by involuntary detrusor contractions that lead to a strong, sudden urge to urinate followed by unintentional leakage. OAB becomes more common with age and is especially prevalent in postmenopausal women.
The pathophysiology involves uninhibited detrusor muscle contractions, often due to impaired inhibitory pathways in the central nervous system or changes in bladder afferent signaling. Oxybutynin is an antimuscarinic agent that blocks M3 receptors in the detrusor muscle, reducing involuntary contractions and increasing bladder capacity. This can significantly improve urgency, frequency, and urge incontinence. It is generally
well-tolerated, although potential side effects include dry mouth, constipation, and blurry vision. In patients with significant lifestyle impairment and low post-void residuals, pharmacologic therapy is the next best step.
Behavioral strategies such as timed voiding and bladder training are first-line, but they can be used in conjunction with pharmacologic therapy or tried first in milder cases. This patient has moderate-to-severe symptoms with lifestyle limitation, making medication an appropriate next step. Her low residual volume rules out overflow, and the absence of infection or prolapse supports a diagnosis of idiopathic detrusor overactivity.
In addition to oxybutynin, other agents such as tolterodine (another muscarinic receptor antagonist) or mirabegron (a beta-3 agonist) can be considered. Mirabegron may be preferred in patients with intolerance to anticholinergic effects, but oxybutynin remains a common and effective option for many patients.
bottom_line: Oxybutynin treats urge incontinence by inhibiting involuntary detrusor contractions. It is a muscarinic receptor antagonist (anti-cholinergic). Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 36-year-old woman comes to the neurology clinic for follow-up regarding multiple sclerosis (MS), which was diagnosed 4 years ago after an episode of optic neuritis and confirmation by MRI. She has been relatively stable on glatiramer acetate and has not had any new lesions or flares in over a year. However, over the past several months, she has developed worsening urinary urgency and frequency. She describes needing to urinate “at least 10 times a day” and often awakens at night with a strong urge to void. On three occasions last month, she was unable to reach the bathroom in time and experienced involuntary urine leakage. She denies dysuria, hematuria, hesitancy, or incomplete emptying. She is sexually active and has no pelvic pain or discharge. Neurologic exam is stable with mild lower extremity spasticity and no new deficits. A urinalysis is unremarkable. Uroflowmetry with postvoid residual shows a bladder volume of 360 mL and postvoid residual of 35 mL. Urodynamic testing reveals detrusor overactivity. She previously tried oxybutynin but discontinued it due to dry mouth and blurry vision. She is eager to find an effective treatment that will not cause intolerable side effects. Which of the following is the next best step in management?
explanation:
Mirabegron is a β3-adrenergic receptor agonist that is used in the treatment of overactive bladder due to detrusor overactivity, particularly when antimuscarinic agents (e.g., oxybutynin, solifenacin) are not tolerated. It works by relaxing the detrusor muscle during the storage phase of micturition, thereby increasing bladder capacity and reducing urinary urgency and incontinence episodes.
This patient has neurogenic bladder dysfunction from multiple sclerosis, and her symptoms are consistent with urge incontinence caused by detrusor overactivity. Importantly, she has already failed first-line antimuscarinic therapy due to anticholinergic side effects. In such patients, mirabegron is the recommended next step, as it avoids cholinergic blockade and has a better side-effect profile, especially in younger individuals or
those susceptible to cognitive effects. Its safety in the setting of normal postvoid residuals also makes it a suitable choice here.
Mirabegron has also gained favor in older patients due to its low risk of cognitive impairment, which can be seen with anticholinergics. In MS-related urge incontinence, maintaining neurologic function and minimizing treatment burden are essential, and this therapy aligns with both goals. Additionally, her stable postvoid residual and absence of obstructive symptoms suggest that mirabegron would be both safe and effective in
her case.
bottom_line: Urge incontinence is common in patients with multiple sclerosis. Oxybutynin, a muscarinic receptor antagonist, and mirabegron, a beta-3 agonist, are common treatments. Baclofen is a GABA-B agonist that treats spasticity in MS. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 69-year-old man comes to the physician because of a 2-month history of progressive fatigue, shortness of breath with mild exertion, and swelling in his lower legs. He has a history of hypertension, type II diabetes, and myocardial infarction 5 years ago. His medications include lisinopril, metoprolol, furosemide, and a recently added agent for symptom control. On exam, his BP is 102/60 mmHg, HR 54, and he has bibasilar crackles with 1+ pitting edema in both ankles. Laboratory results show serum potassium of 5.8 mEq/L and creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL. ECG shows peaked T waves and prolonged PR interval. Which of the following medications is most likely responsible for his current presentation?
explanation:
Eplerenone and spironolactone are both aldosterone receptor antagonists that act as potassium-sparing diuretics, and are frequently used in heart failure. By blocking aldosterone receptors, they interfere with potassium secretion at the cortical collecting duct of the kidney, thereby increasing the risk of hyperkalemia in some patients. This risk is worsening in the setting of renal insufficiency. In this case, creatinine is elevated at
2.1 mg/dL (NR 0.7-1.2). Peaked T-waves on ECG mean hyperkalemia. Spironolactone carries a greater risk than eplerenone of gynecomastia because of its blockade of androgen receptors, resulting in estrogenic effects that predominate.
Amiloride and triamterine are ENaC inhibitors that are also potassium-sparing and carry the same risk of hyperkalemia.
bottom_line: Eplerenone and spironolactone are aldosterone receptor antagonists used sometimes in heart failure. They carry increased risk of hyperkalemia due to interfering with RAAS. Peaked T waves mean hyperkalemia on ECG. Spironolactone has greater risk of gynecomastia than eplerenone. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 26-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his roommate due to confusion, agitation, and muscle stiffness. He has a history of major depressive disorder that has been poorly controlled. His psychiatrist recently switched him from fluoxetine to tranylcypromine. On the day of presentation, he reportedly took both medications along with over-the-counter dextromethorphan for a cough. His temperature is 39.6°C (103.3°F), pulse is 128/min, blood pressure is 168/102 mm Hg, and respirations are 22/min. On examination, the patient is disoriented and diaphoretic with bilateral lower extremity hyperreflexia and inducible ankle clonus. He has tremulous upper limbs and rigid lower limbs. Bowel sounds are hyperactive. Labs are pending. Which of the following best explains this patient’s symptoms?
explanation:
This patient’s presentation is consistent with serotonin syndrome, a potentially life-threatening condition caused by excess serotonergic activity in the CNS. It typically occurs due to serotonergic drug overdose, drug combinations, or pharmacokinetic interactions. This patient took an MAOI (tranylcypromine) shortly after discontinuing an SSRI (fluoxetine), along with dextromethorphan, all of which increase serotonin levels. The
key mechanistic problem is monoamine oxidase inhibition, which prevents the breakdown of serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine, dramatically raising synaptic serotonin levels when combined with other serotonergic agents.
MAOIs are particularly dangerous in this setting because fluoxetine has a long half-life (~5–7 days) and its active metabolite persists for up to 2 weeks. This creates a significant risk for serotonin syndrome if an MAOI is started before fluoxetine has cleared the system. Clinically, serotonin syndrome presents with a triad of mental status changes (confusion, agitation), autonomic instability (fever, hypertension, diaphoresis), and
neuromuscular hyperactivity (clonus, hyperreflexia, tremor, rigidity). The hyperreflexia and clonus are especially important distinguishing features from neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), which is associated with dopamine blockade and presents with lead-pipe rigidity and hyporeflexia.
bottom_line: MAOIs cause serotonin syndrome by preventing serotonin breakdown, especially when combined with SSRIs or other serotonergic drugs. This interaction can be life-threatening due to the accumulation of serotonin in the CNS. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 66-year-old man is hospitalized for management of a diabetic foot infection involving the dorsum of his right foot. He has a long-standing history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus and chronic kidney disease stage III. On presentation, he has fever, leukocytosis, and purulent drainage from a 2cm ulcer overlying the first metatarsal head. Wound cultures reveal a multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa. The inpatient team initiates combination therapy with piperacillin-tazobactam and gentamicin while awaiting antibiotic susceptibility results. On hospital day 2, he reports mild tinnitus and a sensation of unsteadiness when walking to the bathroom. The team suspects a drug-related adverse effect based on his recent medication changes. The gentamicin is discontinued, and supportive care is provided. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of the medication most likely responsible for this patient’s symptoms?

options.A: Binds the 30S ribosomal subunit and inhibits initiation and translocation
options.B: Binds the 50S ribosomal subunit and inhibits peptide bond formation
options.C: Binds DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV
options.D: Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase and blocks tetrahydrofolate synthesis
options.E: Inhibits peptidoglycan cross-linking by binding D-Ala-D-Ala residues

answer_text: Binds the 30S ribosomal subunit and inhibits initiation and translocation
explanation:
Gentamicin is an aminoglycoside antibiotic that exerts its bactericidal action by irreversibly binding the 30S subunit of prokaryotic ribosomes. This interferes with the formation of the initiation complex, causes misreading of mRNA, and inhibits translocation during protein synthesis. These effects result in production of defective proteins and ultimate bacterial cell death.
Gentamicin is especially active against aerobic gram-negative organisms, including Pseudomonas, and is used in serious infections such as diabetic foot ulcers. However, because aminoglycosides accumulate in the renal cortex and inner ear, they can cause nephrotoxicity and vestibulocochlear (CN VIII) toxicity, especially in patients with impaired renal function.
Aminoglycosides can also cause acute tubular necrosis.
bottom_line: Aminoglycosides inhibit the 30S ribosomal subunit. They are ototoxic and nephrotoxic. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 68-year-old woman presents to the clinic due to gradually worsening motor symptoms over the past month. She reports a resting tremor in her hands, stiffness in her limbs, and a general sense of slowness that has made it increasingly difficult to perform daily activities like cooking and buttoning clothes. Her daughter notes that she has developed a “blank stare,” has stopped smiling as much, and walks with reduced arm swing. The patient denies falls, cognitive changes, hallucinations, or mood symptoms. She has no personal or family history of neurodegenerative disease. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus complicated by gastroparesis, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Current medications include insulin glargine, lisinopril, simvastatin, low-dose aspirin, and metoclopramide. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, she appears expressionless with reduced blinking and a mildly stooped posture. She has cogwheel rigidity in both upper extremities, decreased arm swing bilaterally, and a 4-Hz resting tremor in her right hand. Gait is slow and shuffling. Muscle strength and deep tendon reflexes are normal, and sensation is intact. Mental status is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
This patient presents with parkinsonian features—bradykinesia, resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, and masked facies—in the context of chronic metoclopramide use. These findings are consistent with drug-induced parkinsonism (DIP), which occurs when dopamine D2 receptors in the nigrostriatal pathway are blocked. Metoclopramide, although primarily used as a gastrointestinal prokinetic and antiemetic, crosses the bloodbrain barrier and antagonizes central dopamine receptors, thereby impairing the normal function of basal ganglia circuits responsible for initiating and regulating movement.
The pathophysiology is similar to that of antipsychotic-induced parkinsonism. Both classes of drugs—metoclopramide and dopamine antagonist antipsychotics like haloperidol or risperidone—inhibit D2 receptors in the striatum. This disrupts the balance between dopaminergic and cholinergic activity, leading to relative cholinergic excess and resultant extrapyramidal symptoms. Symptoms typically emerge gradually over weeks
to months and are often symmetric but can be asymmetric, as in this case. Elderly patients, particularly women, are at increased risk.
The most appropriate next step is to discontinue metoclopramide, which removes the underlying cause of dopamine blockade. In many cases, this alone leads to full resolution of symptoms over several weeks. Continuing the medication may worsen the parkinsonism or lead to irreversible syndromes such as tardive dyskinesia. Dopaminergic therapy is typically not effective or necessary in DIP and may cause undesirable side
effects.
bottom_line: Metoclopramide can cause reversible parkinsonism due to D2 receptor blockade in the basal ganglia. The mechanism is identical to that of dopamine-blocking antipsychotics. In patients with new-onset parkinsonian symptoms and known exposure to dopamine antagonists, the first step is to discontinue the offending agent, not to initiate symptomatic or dopaminergic therapy. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 64-year-old man comes to the clinic for evaluation of tremors in his hands that have progressively worsened over the past 8 months. He says the tremor is most noticeable when writing, drinking coffee, or holding tools while doing household repairs. He reports spilling water from glasses and occasionally notices a mild tremor of his voice. The tremor does not occur when he is at rest. He does not drink alcohol. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. Vital signs are normal. On neurologic examination, there is a fine bilateral tremor of the hands when his arms are outstretched, which worsens with goal-directed movements (e.g., finger-to-nose testing). Muscle tone, strength, deep tendon reflexes, and gait are all within normal limits. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?
explanation:
This patient presents with hallmark features of essential tremor (ET)—a progressive, bilateral action tremor of the upper extremities that is exacerbated by goal-directed movements such as writing, eating, or using tools. ET may also involve the head and voice but spares the legs. The tremor is classically absent at rest and worsens with movement or when maintaining posture, distinguishing it from Parkinson disease.
Neurologic examination in ET is otherwise normal, including muscle strength, reflexes, tone, and gait, which is consistent with the findings in this case. The tremor can significantly impact quality of life and functional ability, justifying pharmacologic treatment when it becomes disabling. Although many patients report improvement in tremor with alcohol consumption, this is not universal or diagnostic, and its absence does not rule
out ET.
Propranolol, a nonselective beta-blocker, is the first-line treatment for essential tremor. It reduces tremor amplitude by antagonizing peripheral beta-2 adrenergic receptors involved in muscle spindle sensitivity, and may have central effects on oscillatory thalamic activity. Its effectiveness is particularly well established in patients with upper limb tremor that interferes with daily activities. Alternatives to propranolol include primidone
(a barbiturate), which may be used as monotherapy or in combination. However, due to potential sedation and side effects with primidone—especially in older adults—propranolol is often tried first unless contraindicated. Other options, such as topiramate or gabapentin, are generally considered second- or third-line. In patients refractory to medications, deep brain stimulation may be considered.
bottom_line: Essential tremor is an action tremor that worsens with movement and improves at rest. It commonly affects the hands and can interfere with daily tasks. Propranolol is the first-line pharmacologic treatment. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 19-year-old college student is brought to the emergency department by her roommate after an intentional drug overdose. Earlier that evening, the patient had an emotional breakdown after a heated argument with her long-term partner, during which the partner abruptly ended the relationship. The roommate reports that after the argument, the patient went into her room and remained there for nearly an hour. When the roommate knocked to check on her, the patient eventually opened the door tearfully and admitted to having swallowed “a handful of Tylenol” in an attempt to harm herself. She said she had taken the pills all at once approximately 3–4 hours ago, but could not recall the exact quantity. The roommate immediately called emergency services. The patient denies any other co-ingestions and has not consumed alcohol or other substances today. She has no known psychiatric diagnoses, but the roommate says she has seemed increasingly withdrawn over the past two weeks, rarely leaving the dorm room and skipping meals. On arrival at the ED, the patient is awake and oriented but appears emotionally distressed. She is crying intermittently, avoids eye contact, and speaks softly but coherently. She denies chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, or abdominal pain. She has not vomited since the ingestion. She has no past medical history, no allergies, and takes no medications. She does not smoke, drink, or use recreational drugs. Her last menstrual period was 2 weeks ago. Vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals a thin but well-nourished woman in mild emotional distress. Sclerae are anicteric. Abdominal examination is soft and non-tender without hepatomegaly. There are no skin findings, needle marks, or signs of trauma. Mental status is appropriate with no suicidal ideation at this time. Initial labs reveal normal electrolytes, glucose, and renal function. Liver function tests show an AST of 42 U/L and ALT of 48 U/L. Total bilirubin is 0.7 mg/dL, and INR is 1.0. A serum acetaminophen level drawn exactly 4 hours after ingestion is 195 µg/mL. Psychiatry has been consulted and will evaluate the patient once medical stabilization is ensured. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
This patient presents within the 4-hour window after a single, intentional acetaminophen overdose, and her serum acetaminophen concentration is 195 µg/mL, measured precisely 4 hours post-ingestion. This level places her well above the treatment threshold on the Rumack-Matthew nomogram, which is the validated tool for assessing hepatotoxicity risk following acute ingestion. The treatment line on the nomogram begins at
150 µg/mL at 4 hours, meaning any level above that necessitates antidotal therapy with N-acetylcysteine (NAC).
Although this patient currently has normal liver function tests and is asymptomatic, that does not rule out impending liver injury. In fact, hepatic damage from acetaminophen toxicity often becomes biochemically and clinically apparent 12–24 hours after ingestion. The purpose of NAC is to prevent hepatotoxicity by replenishing glutathione, the hepatic compound that detoxifies NAPQI (N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine) — a toxic
metabolite of acetaminophen. When glutathione stores are depleted, NAPQI accumulates and binds to hepatocytes, causing centrilobular necrosis and potentially fulminant hepatic failure. NAC works best when administered within 8 hours of ingestion, with near-100% prevention of hepatic injury when given early.
Additionally, NAC improves outcomes even when administered in the later stages, including patients with hepatic failure. Because the patient’s timing is known, the dose can be accurately interpreted using the nomogram. NAC should be started immediately and continued for the full treatment course (usually 21-hour IV or 72-hour oral regimens), while monitoring liver enzymes and coagulation parameters.
bottom_line: In a patient with a known time of acetaminophen ingestion, use the Rumack-Matthew nomogram to determine the need for N-acetylcysteine (NAC). If the level at ≥4 hours post-ingestion is above the treatment line (150 µg/mL at 4 hours), start NAC immediately. Do not wait for symptoms or rising transaminases. Early NAC prevents hepatotoxicity and improves outcomes. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 55-year-old woman presents to her general practitioner's office for a health maintenance examination. After discussion with her physician, the addition of an insulin secretagogue is considered for management of her type II diabetes that acts via blocking potassium channels. Which of the following is the most appropriate medication as per this discussion with her physician?
explanation: Repaglinide is an insulin secretagogue (i.e., promotes insulin release) in the class of drugs called meglitinides. These have an MOA similar to sulfonylureas (e.g., glyburide). The MOA is: close ATP-sensitive potassium channels on the beta-islet cells → K+ builds up in the cell → depolarization → calcium influx occurs → increased efflux of insulin vesicles. This MOA is HY for both the meglitinides and sulfonylureas.
bottom_line: Repaglinide (a meglitinide) and glyburide (a sulfonylurea) promote insulin release by blocking the β-islet cell ATP-sensitive K+ channel. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 56-year-old man comes to the clinic for management of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus despite adherence to dietary modifications and regular exercise for the past 6 months. He was diagnosed 2 years ago and initially managed with metformin, which he tolerates well. His medical history is notable for hypertension and dyslipidemia. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. He has no history of hypoglycemic episodes. His current medications are metformin, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. Physical examination shows a BMI of 31 kg/m² and blood pressure of 134/78 mm Hg. Fundoscopic examination reveals no diabetic retinopathy. Neurologic examination is unremarkable. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin A1c of 8.2%, fasting plasma glucose of 165 mg/dL, and a serum creatinine of 0.8 mg/dL. The decision is made to add an oral hypoglycemic agent that increases pancreatic β-cell insulin secretion by binding to and inhibiting ATP-dependent potassium channels on the β-cell membrane. Which of the following is the most appropriate medication to add to this patient’s regimen?
explanation:
This patient has poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) despite lifestyle modifications and metformin therapy, with an A1c of 8.2%. In T2DM, the primary pathology is insulin resistance combined with progressive β-cell dysfunction leading to impaired insulin secretion. Patients are often asymptomatic early in the disease, but classic symptoms—polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia, weight loss, and blurry vision—may appear
as hyperglycemia worsens. Diagnosis is confirmed by laboratory criteria such as fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL, 2-hour oral glucose tolerance test ≥200 mg/dL, or hemoglobin A1c ≥6.5%.
The most appropriate next step is to add a sulfonylurea, such as glyburide, to enhance insulin secretion. Sulfonylureas act on the ATP-sensitive potassium (K_ATP) channels located on the membranes of pancreatic β cells. Normally, glucose metabolism increases the ATP/ADP ratio in β cells, leading to closure of these K_ATP channels, membrane depolarization, opening of voltage-gated calcium channels, and calcium influx—
this triggers exocytosis of insulin-containing vesicles. Sulfonylureas mimic this effect by directly binding to the SUR1 subunit of K_ATP channels, causing their closure independent of glucose levels, which increases intracellular calcium and promotes insulin release.
bottom_line: Sulfonylureas MOA. Sulfonylureas are particularly effective in patients who still have some residual β-cell function. They are classified into first-generation (eg, chlorpropamide, tolbutamide) and second-generation agents (eg, glyburide, glipizide, glimepiride), with the latter being more potent and having fewer side effects unrelated to hypoglycemia. Common side effects include hypoglycemia—especially in elderly patients or those with renal insufficiency—and weight gain due to increased insulin activity. Rare adverse effects include hyponatremia (chlorpropamide), disulfiram-like reactions (first-generation), and gastrointestinal upset. Management of T2DM focuses on glycemic control to prevent microvascular complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) and aggressive cardiovascular risk reduction to lower macrovascular complications (myocardial infarction, stroke, peripheral artery disease). First-line therapy includes lifestyle modifications and metformin. If A1c remains above target, second-line agents are chosen based on patient-specific factors —sulfonylureas like glyburide are cost-effective and potent but carry a higher risk of hypoglycemia compared to DPP-4 inhibitors, GLP-1 receptor agonists, or SGLT2 inhibitors.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 46-year-old woman comes to the hematology clinic for follow-up after completing her first cycle of chemotherapy for a recently diagnosed lymphoid malignancy. Six months ago, she began experiencing fatigue, drenching night sweats, and an unintentional 7-kg (15-lb) weight loss. She also noted swelling in her neck and underarms that gradually increased in size. She has no history of autoimmune disease. She does not smoke, drink alcohol, or use illicit drugs. There is no family history of cancer. On physical examination at diagnosis, she had multiple firm, nontender cervical and axillary lymph nodes. Hepatosplenomegaly was present without abdominal tenderness. Laboratory studies revealed mild normocytic anemia and an elevated lactate dehydrogenase level. Peripheral blood smear showed no abnormal cells. CT scans demonstrated diffuse lymphadenopathy above and below the diaphragm. An excisional biopsy of a cervical lymph node revealed sheets of atypical lymphoid cells with irregular nuclear contours and scant cytoplasm. Immunohistochemistry was positive for CD19 and CD20, but negative for CD3 and CD15. Based on these findings, she was started on combination chemotherapy including an alkylating agent, an anthracycline, a vinca alkaloid, and rituximab. After the first infusion of the monoclonal antibody, she experienced transient fever, chills, and mild hypotension, which resolved with supportive care. She was counseled that these infusion reactions are common and usually diminish with subsequent doses. Which of the following is the molecular target of this patient’s monoclonal antibody therapy?
explanation:
Rituximab is a chimeric monoclonal antibody that specifically targets CD20, a surface antigen expressed on pre-B cells and mature B lymphocytes but absent on plasma cells and hematopoietic stem cells. By binding CD20, rituximab triggers immune-mediated destruction of B cells through complement-dependent cytotoxicity and antibody-dependent cell-mediated cytotoxicity (ADCC) by NK cells and macrophages. The
depletion of CD20-positive cells is useful in treating B-cell malignancies (eg, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia) and certain autoimmune diseases (eg, rheumatoid arthritis, pemphigus vulgaris).
Because B cells are crucial for humoral immunity, rituximab increases the risk of infections, particularly serious bacterial infections such as bacterial pneumonia. Over time, hypogammaglobulinemia can develop due to the loss of B-cell precursors, reducing the ability to mount antibody-mediated responses against encapsulated organisms (eg, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae). Viral reactivations, especially
hepatitis B virus, and opportunistic infections like progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML) from JC virus have also been reported. Patients should be evaluated for hepatitis B prior to therapy and monitored for infection symptoms during treatment.
In this patient, CD20 positivity on biopsy guided the use of rituximab to specifically target malignant B cells while sparing T cells and other non–B-lineage immune cells. The combination with cytotoxic chemotherapy enhances tumor kill but necessitates careful infection prevention, vaccination (prior to therapy if possible), and prompt evaluation of febrile illnesses. The infusion reaction she experienced after the first dose is
common due to cytokine release from rapid tumor cell lysis but typically lessens with subsequent infusions.
bottom_line: Rituximab targets CD20 on B lymphocytes, leading to immune-mediated B-cell depletion. This increases susceptibility to serious bacterial infections—especially bacterial pneumonia. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: An 82-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-day history of severe pain, redness, and swelling in his right great toe. The pain began abruptly at night and worsened over the next 48 hours, making it difficult for him to walk. He has a history of hypertension, chronic kidney disease, and hyperlipidemia. He takes lisinopril, atorvastatin, and low-dose aspirin daily. Two years ago, he had a similar episode in his left foot that resolved with treatment. On examination, the metatarsophalangeal joint of the right great toe is markedly tender, warm, and erythematous with restricted range of motion. Synovial fluid aspiration is performed, and the patient is started on colchicine. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of this medication in reducing his symptoms?

options.A: Decreased leukocyte motility and infiltration into the joint
options.B: Decreased proliferation of synovial lining cells
options.C: Increased histamine release from local mast cells
options.D: Increased renal excretion of uric acid
options.E: Inhibition of monosodium urate crystal formation in the synovium

answer_text: Decreased leukocyte motility and infiltration into the joint
explanation:
This patient’s presentation—acute monoarticular arthritis of the first metatarsophalangeal joint with intense erythema, swelling, and tenderness—along with a past history of similar self-limited episodes, strongly suggests acute gouty arthritis. Risk factors such as advanced age, chronic kidney disease (reducing urate clearance), and aspirin use (which can elevate uric acid levels) further support the diagnosis. In this context,
colchicine is an appropriate choice for acute management when NSAIDs are contraindicated or undesirable. The drug’s main therapeutic effect in gout comes from limiting the movement of inflammatory cells into the affected joint by reducing leukocyte motility and infiltration.
Colchicine works by binding to tubulin and inhibiting microtubule polymerization, which disrupts several microtubule-dependent functions in neutrophils, including chemotaxis, phagocytosis, and degranulation. This directly reduces neutrophil recruitment to the site of monosodium urate crystal deposition and limits the release of inflammatory mediators. Colchicine also inhibits activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome, decreasing
interleukin-1β production—critical in crystal-induced inflammation. While most effective when given early in an attack, colchicine’s use is limited by a narrow therapeutic window. Common adverse effects include gastrointestinal upset (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) due to its effects on rapidly dividing intestinal epithelium, while rare but serious toxicities include myopathy, bone marrow suppression, and multi-organ failure—
particularly in elderly or renally impaired patients.
Gout is an inflammatory arthritis caused by monosodium urate crystal precipitation within joints when serum uric acid exceeds its solubility threshold (~6.8 mg/dL). Hyperuricemia can result from overproduction—as seen in myeloproliferative disorders, tumor lysis syndrome, Lesch-Nyhan syndrome, high-purine diets, or PRPP synthetase overactivity—or from underexcretion due to chronic kidney disease, diuretic use, lead
nephropathy, or dehydration. Crystals are recognized by innate immune cells, especially macrophages, triggering inflammasome activation and IL-1β release, which recruits neutrophils. These neutrophils then perpetuate inflammation by phagocytosing crystals, releasing lysosomal enzymes, and producing reactive oxygen species, worsening tissue injury.
Chronic gout arises when recurrent acute attacks go untreated, leading to tophi, chronic synovitis, joint destruction, and urate deposition in soft tissues and kidneys. The diagnosis of gout is confirmed by identification of needle-shaped, negatively birefringent monosodium urate crystals under polarized light microscopy from synovial fluid aspiration, a key distinction from pseudogout, which shows positively birefringent calcium
pyrophosphate crystals. Long-term management aims to keep serum uric acid levels below the crystallization threshold using urate-lowering drugs (eg, allopurinol, febuxostat, probenecid). In contrast, acute management focuses on dampening inflammation through NSAIDs, colchicine, or glucocorticoids. Patient comorbidities guide drug choice; in this elderly patient with chronic kidney disease, colchicine is an effective
alternative to NSAIDs if dosing is carefully adjusted to avoid toxicity.
bottom_line: Colchicine relieves acute gout symptoms by binding tubulin, inhibiting microtubule polymerization, and reducing neutrophil chemotaxis, activation, and infiltration into the joint—disrupting the inflammatory cascade without affecting uric acid levels. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 42-year-old man presents for follow-up after being diagnosed with HIV infection 4 months ago. He reports that since starting his new medication regimen, he has noticed significant changes in his body. He says his face has become rounder, and he has gained fat in his upper back while losing fat in his arms and legs. He also mentions that his clothes no longer fit properly, and he feels self-conscious about the redistribution of his body fat. He has also been more fatigued and sometimes feels unusually thirsty. He has no history of opportunistic infections, and he has been compliant with his therapy. His other past medical history is notable only for well-controlled hypertension. He works as an accountant and does not use alcohol, tobacco, or illicit drugs. He is in a stable relationship and practices safe sex. On exam, he is afebrile, and his vital signs are normal. He has a rounded “moon face” and fat accumulation over the posterior neck. His extremities appear thinner compared to central areas. Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams are normal. Abdominal exam reveals no organomegaly. Neurological exam is intact with no focal deficits. Laboratory workup from his last visit noted elevated fasting glucose. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of the medication causing this patient's presentation?

options.A: Binding to gp41 to block viral entry into host cells
options.B: Inhibiting HIV integrase to prevent incorporation of viral DNA into host genome
options.C: Inhibiting protease activity to prevent cleavage of viral polyproteins
options.D: Inhibiting reverse transcriptase to prevent conversion of viral RNA into DNA
options.E: Inhibiting cytochrome P450 to increase serum levels of other protease inhibitors

answer_text: Inhibiting protease activity to prevent cleavage of viral polyproteins
explanation:
Protease inhibitors, such as ritonavir, block the action of HIV-1 protease, an enzyme essential for viral maturation. Normally, protease cleaves long viral polyproteins into smaller, functional proteins that are then assembled into an infectious virion. Without this cleavage, immature and noninfectious viral particles are produced and released, preventing further spread of infection. This makes protease inhibitors effective at a late
stage of the viral life cycle and an important part of combination antiretroviral therapy.
A notable aspect of protease inhibitors is their metabolic side effects. They can cause insulin resistance and hyperglycemia, predisposing patients to diabetes. They are also associated with dyslipidemia and abnormal fat redistribution, leading to a “Cushingoid” appearance with moon facies, dorsocervical fat pad (“buffalo hump”), and central fat accumulation. At the same time, there may be peripheral fat wasting in the limbs.
These effects can be distressing for patients and impact adherence, but recognizing them is key to counseling and management.
Despite these adverse effects, protease inhibitors remain a cornerstone of HIV therapy due to their potency and ability to suppress viral replication by halting protease-mediated cleavage. Their mechanism is distinct from reverse transcriptase inhibitors and integrase inhibitors, which act earlier in the viral cycle. Clinicians balance the benefits of viral suppression against the metabolic risks, often managing the latter with lifestyle
modification or adjunctive therapy.
bottom_line: HIV protease inhibitors like ritonavir can cause Cushingoid appearance and insulin resistance. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 26-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his wife after being found sitting on the back porch of the house confused and unresponsive. On arrival, he is diaphoretic and drooling, with 1mm pupils, wheezing, and diffuse muscle fasciculations. Blood pressure is 90/60. HR is 50. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacologic therapy for this patient's condition?
explanation:
Organophosphate poisoning results from inhibition of acetylcholinesterase, leading to excess acetylcholine within the synaptic cleft and resultant cholinergic toxicity. In other words, organophosphates are cholinesterase inhibitors.
The USMLE will classically give you a vignette of someone picking fruit on a farm, or someone who drank a fluid in a suicide attempt, but you can't rely on these vignettes as buzzy presentations. Our Q here clearly gives a pro-cholinergic presentation, where you can infer atropine, an anti-cholinergic is the answer to treat it.
If an agent is cholinergic, it will cause DUMBBELSS signs/symptoms –> Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bradycardia, Bronchoconstriction, neuromuscular Excitation, Lacrimation, Salivation, Sweating.
If an agent is anti-cholinergic, it will cause the opposite of DUMBBELSS, or “anti-DUMBBELSS” –> Constipation, Urinary retention, Mydriasis, Tachycardia, Bronchodilation (in reality, β2 agonism, but not M antagonism, will bronchodilate), Skeletal muscle relaxation, Dry eye, Dry mouth, Anhydrosis.
Atropine is the first-line treatment and works by blocking muscarinic receptors.
Pralidoxime is given after atropine and works by regenerating acetylcholinesterase.
USMLE wants you to know that wearing gloves is the answer over wearing a mask in order to prevent - i.e., organophosphate poisoning mostly occurs through the skin, rather than by inhalation, which is presumably why backpackers picking fruit is a vignette they like.
bottom_line: Organophosphates are cholinesterase inhibitors and cause DUMBBELSS. Treatment is atropine followed by pralidoxime. USMLE wants gloves over a mask for prevention. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 58-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician for follow-up after hospital discharge. She was recently admitted with chest pain and diagnosed with a non-ST elevation myocardial infarction. During her hospitalization, she was started on aspirin, a statin, a beta blocker, and clopidogrel that she has now been told she must continue long term. The patient has a remarkable personal history outside of her health. She grew up in the northeastern United States and developed a deep love for outdoor activities. From her late teens onward, she spent weekends kayaking and canoeing across various lakes in New Hampshire, Vermont, and upstate New York. She loved the quiet mist rising from the water at sunrise, the sight of loons diving beneath the surface, and the feeling of paddling alone in crisp autumn air. She joined a small group of outdoor enthusiasts who would explore remote waterways, sometimes portaging their canoes through rugged trails to reach hidden lakes. Over time, she became skilled at navigating whitewater stretches and learned rescue techniques for overturned boats. By her mid-30s, she had visited nearly every major lake across the region, carefully recording details of each trip in a journal. By age 40, however, she began longing for new horizons. She believed the northeastern lakes, though beautiful, no longer offered the sense of adventure she craved. At age 43, she made a life-changing decision to relocate to Alaska, where she could pursue kayaking on a grander scale. There, she discovered vast glacier-fed lakes, icy rivers, and waterways surrounded by snowcapped peaks. She recalls paddling across Lake Clark, where the turquoise water reflected towering mountains, and she camped on the shoreline beneath endless summer twilight. On another trip, she ventured into Prince William Sound, maneuvering her kayak among floating icebergs while spotting seals resting on the ice. These experiences deepened her passion and solidified her reputation among friends as someone who had dedicated her life to the water. In Alaska, she also took up photography, capturing dramatic images of her canoe at dawn against backdrops of jagged peaks. She began writing essays about her experiences for local magazines, describing the solitude, the rhythm of paddling, and the way each lake had its own personality. Though she sometimes traveled back east to visit family, she felt most at home among Alaska’s lakes and rivers. Unfortunately, her health began to shift in recent years. She developed hypertension in her early 50s and was prescribed lisinopril. Two years later, she was found to have high cholesterol and was advised to begin statin therapy. Despite lifestyle adjustments, she recently suffered a myocardial infarction, leading to her current medication regimen. Today, she feels well but wants to understand her medications better. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of this patient’s clopidogrel therapy?

options.A: Binds glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptors to prevent fibrinogen cross-linking
options.B: Inhibits adenosine diphosphate (ADP) P2Y₁₂ receptor on platelets
options.C: Inhibits cyclooxygenase to block thromboxane A₂ synthesis
options.D: Increases cyclic AMP by phosphodiesterase inhibition
options.E: Promotes antithrombin activity to inactivate thrombin and factor Xa

answer_text: Inhibits adenosine diphosphate (ADP) P2Y₁₂ receptor on platelets
explanation:
When you get obnoxiously long question stems on USMLE, read the last line first. You can answer this question by merely reading the last line, rather than spending 42 minutes getting pulled into this patient's hysteria of traversing cascading lakes.
Clopidogrel is a thienopyridine antiplatelet drug that irreversibly blocks the P2Y₁₂ subtype of ADP receptors on the platelet surface. Normally, ADP binds to this receptor, activating glycoprotein IIb/IIIa receptors and promoting fibrinogen-mediated platelet cross-linking. By preventing this signaling pathway, clopidogrel inhibits platelet activation and aggregation, reducing the risk of arterial thrombosis. This is crucial in patients with
acute coronary syndromes, recent myocardial infarction, or following stent placement.
The mechanism of clopidogrel is distinct from that of aspirin, which blocks cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) and prevents thromboxane A₂ formation. Because these two drugs act through different pathways, they are often used together for dual antiplatelet therapy, providing synergistic protection against clot formation. Clopidogrel’s effect is irreversible and lasts for the lifespan of the platelet (7–10 days), which is important for clinical
planning in surgical procedures where bleeding risk is a concern.
Other thienopyridines such as prasugrel and ticlopidine, and newer non-thienopyridines like ticagrelor, also act on the P2Y₁₂ receptor. Together, these drugs form a cornerstone of therapy for patients undergoing percutaneous coronary intervention and for those with acute coronary syndromes. In this patient, clopidogrel’s mechanism directly complements aspirin to prevent further ischemic events and stent thrombosis.
bottom_line: Clopidogrel irreversibly inhibits the ADP P2Y₁₂ receptor on platelets, preventing activation of glycoprotein IIb/IIIa and reducing platelet aggregation. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 27-year-old woman presents with painful vesicular lesions on her lower lip that appeared after a week of fever and sore throat. She reports similar outbreaks in the past, usually triggered by stress or illness. Physical examination reveals clusters of small vesicles on an erythematous base along the vermillion border. A swab from the lesion confirms herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection. She is prescribed valacyclovir. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of this therapy?

options.A: Competitive inhibition of viral DNA polymerase with chain termination after incorporation
options.B: Direct inhibition of host DNA polymerase alpha, halting DNA replication in human cells
options.C: Formation of stable DNA polymerase–DNA complexes preventing strand separation
options.D: Inhibition of proofreading exonuclease activity of viral DNA polymerase
options.E: Reversible allosteric inhibition of viral DNA polymerase without chain termination

answer_text: Competitive inhibition of viral DNA polymerase with chain termination after incorporation
explanation:
Acyclovir/valacyclovir is a guanosine analog that must first be phosphorylated by viral thymidine kinase, which ensures that the drug is activated primarily in infected cells. Host kinases then convert it to acyclovir triphosphate, the active form. This metabolite acts as a substrate analog for viral DNA polymerase, competitively inhibiting the enzyme and incorporating into the viral DNA chain. Because acyclovir lacks a 3′ hydroxyl
group, further elongation cannot occur, leading to premature chain termination.
This dual mechanism — competitive inhibition plus chain termination — explains acyclovir’s potency against HSV and varicella-zoster virus. The reliance on viral thymidine kinase also explains why it has minimal toxicity to host cells and why acyclovir-resistant HSV strains usually carry mutations in the viral thymidine kinase or viral DNA polymerase. Importantly, acyclovir has no effect on host DNA polymerases, making it highly
selective for infected cells.
bottom_line: Acyclovir is a guanosine analog activated by viral thymidine kinase. It works by competitively inhibiting viral DNA polymerase and terminating the DNA chain after incorporation. Its selectivity for virally infected cells explains its efficacy against HSV and VZV with minimal host toxicity. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action)

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 52-year-old man is evaluated in the emergency department after undergoing abdominal surgery 2 days ago. Since the procedure, he has not passed stool or flatus, and his abdomen feels distended. On exam, bowel sounds are hypoactive, and there is mild diffuse tenderness without rebound or guarding. He has no nausea or vomiting at present, but he reports discomfort due to abdominal bloating. The patient has no history of cardiovascular disease, asthma, or glaucoma. He is started on bethanechol. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of this pharmacotherapy?

options.A: Direct agonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors
options.B: Inhibition of acetylcholinesterase at the synaptic cleft
options.C: Indirect agonism by stimulating presynaptic acetylcholine release
options.E: Reversible competitive antagonist of muscarinic receptors

answer_text: Direct agonist at muscarinic acetylcholine receptors
explanation:
Bethanechol is a direct muscarinic acetylcholine receptor agonist. It mimics the action of endogenous acetylcholine by binding directly to muscarinic receptors located in smooth muscle, exocrine glands, and the heart. In the gastrointestinal tract, its stimulation of muscarinic receptors promotes contraction of intestinal smooth muscle, enhancing peristalsis and facilitating relief of ileus. Similarly, in the bladder, muscarinic receptor
stimulation increases detrusor muscle tone and relaxes the sphincter, allowing improved voiding in urinary retention. Unlike other cholinergic drugs, bethanechol has minimal activity at nicotinic receptors, making it selective for parasympathetic end-organ effects.
Importantly, bethanechol is not broken down by acetylcholinesterase, giving it a longer duration of action than acetylcholine itself. This property allows it to be clinically useful for conditions like postoperative ileus and non-obstructive urinary retention. Because it acts directly on muscarinic receptors, its effect is not dependent on presynaptic nerve activity or acetylcholine release. This differentiates it from indirect cholinomimetics
such as neostigmine, which work by inhibiting acetylcholinesterase and prolonging endogenous acetylcholine action. Clinicians must be cautious when prescribing bethanechol, as excessive muscarinic stimulation can cause side effects such as bradycardia, bronchospasm, abdominal cramping, and excessive salivation.
Bethanechol is a prototypical muscarinic agonist that highlights the principle of direct cholinergic stimulation in therapy. It has little to no central nervous system penetration because it is a quaternary amine, limiting its effects to peripheral tissues. On USMLE questions, its use is classically associated with urinary retention (particularly postpartum or postoperatively) and ileus. Recognizing its direct receptor agonism is crucial for
distinguishing it from other drugs that act indirectly or that work at different cholinergic receptor subtypes.
bottom_line: Bethanechol is a direct muscarinic receptor agonist that stimulates smooth muscle contraction in the gut and bladder. It must be distinguished from indirect cholinomimetics, nicotinic agonists, and muscarinic antagonists, which have different mechanisms and clinical applications. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 58-year-old man comes for follow-up evaluation after recent adjustments to his cardiovascular risk management. He has a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and long-standing hyperlipidemia. Despite adherence to a low-saturated-fat diet and several months of maximally tolerated statin therapy, his most recent fasting lipid panel revealed persistently elevated low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol. He does not drink alcohol regularly, denies smoking, and has no family history of premature myocardial infarction. He reports occasional bloating but denies steatorrhea, diarrhea, or weight loss. On examination, his blood pressure is 128/74 mmHg, pulse 78/min, and BMI is 29 kg/m². Physical examination shows no xanthomas, xanthelasmas, or corneal arcus. Cardiac and abdominal exams are normal. His physician prescribes ezetimibe. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of this newly prescribed drug?

options.A: Binds bile acids in the intestinal lumen to increase fecal loss
options.B: Directly inhibits cholesterol absorption at the brush border of enterocytes
options.C: Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase in hepatocytes to decrease cholesterol synthesis
options.D: Inhibits microsomal triglyceride transfer protein, reducing VLDL secretion
options.E: Upregulates lipoprotein lipase activity to increase triglyceride clearance

answer_text: Directly inhibits cholesterol absorption at the brush border of enterocytes
explanation:
Ezetimibe is a lipid-lowering drug that works by directly inhibiting cholesterol absorption at the brush border of enterocytes in the small intestine. Its target is the Niemann-Pick C1-like 1 (NPC1L1) transporter, which normally facilitates uptake of dietary and biliary cholesterol. By blocking this transporter, ezetimibe reduces the amount of cholesterol delivered to the liver via chylomicron remnants, thereby decreasing hepatic
cholesterol stores. In response, hepatocytes increase expression of LDL receptors, leading to enhanced clearance of circulating LDL cholesterol from the plasma.
This mechanism makes ezetimibe particularly effective in isolated LDL reduction, especially when patients are unable to reach LDL targets with statins alone. Statins reduce cholesterol synthesis by inhibiting HMG-CoA reductase, while ezetimibe reduces intestinal absorption—making the two drugs complementary. When combined, they produce a synergistic effect, lowering LDL cholesterol more than either agent alone.
Ezetimibe is frequently added as a second-line agent for patients with high cardiovascular risk who cannot tolerate high-intensity statins or who require additional LDL lowering beyond what statins achieve.
Clinically, ezetimibe is used in familial hypercholesterolemia, mixed dyslipidemia, and in patients with established atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease who need aggressive lipid control. It has the advantage of being orally administered, well tolerated, and relatively inexpensive. Because it has minimal effect on triglycerides and HDL, it is not used primarily for hypertriglyceridemia or low HDL states. It can also be considered in
statin-intolerant patients, although its effect on cardiovascular outcomes is smaller than that of statins.
Adverse effects of ezetimibe are generally mild, with the most common being gastrointestinal upset such as diarrhea or bloating. Some patients may experience elevated liver transaminases, particularly when ezetimibe is used in combination with statins, necessitating periodic monitoring of liver function tests. Rare side effects include myalgias and arthralgias. Importantly, ezetimibe does not increase the risk of gallstones when
used alone, unlike bile acid sequestrants, and it avoids the systemic toxicities associated with fibrates or niacin.
bottom_line: Ezetimibe blocks the NPC1L1 transporter in intestinal enterocytes, reducing cholesterol absorption, lowering hepatic cholesterol, and upregulating LDL receptors — used as an adjunct to statins for additional LDL lowering, with mild GI and hepatic side effects. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 35-year-old man presents with 1 week of bloody diarrhea, abdominal pain, and tenesmus after returning from a business trip to Mexico. He reports eating street food and drinking local tap water. Vital signs show low-grade fever and mild tachycardia. Examination reveals diffuse abdominal tenderness, and labs show mild leukocytosis and elevated liver enzymes. Stool microscopy reveals trophozoites with ingested red blood cells, and colonoscopy shows flask-shaped ulcers. Abdominal imaging reveals a single hypodense lesion in the right hepatic lobe consistent with a liver abscess. The most appropriate pharmacologic therapy for this condition most likely effectuates pathogen killing via which of the following mechanisms?

options.B: Binds to ergosterol and disrupts fungal cell membrane integrity
options.C: Disrupts DNA by generating free radicals
options.D: Inhibits dihydropteroate synthase and blocks folate synthesis
options.E: Inhibits DNA gyrase and topoisomerase IV

answer_text: Disrupts DNA by generating free radicals
explanation:
Entamoeba histolytica is a protozoan parasite that causes invasive intestinal amebiasis, leading to bloody diarrhea, flask-shaped ulcers in the colon, and liver abscesses due to hematogenous spread. The organism is acquired via the fecal-oral route, especially in areas with poor sanitation or through consumption of contaminated water. Diagnosis is made by identifying trophozoites with ingested erythrocytes in stool, aka
erythrophagocytosis, which is pathognomonic. Extra-intestinal disease, such as liver abscesses, is common and typically presents as RUQ pain with fever.
Treatment on USMLE is metronidazole, which is effective against the invasive trophozoite form, followed by an intra-luminal agent like paromomycin or iodoquinol to clear cysts from the gut. Metronidazole works by entering anaerobic organisms and forming toxic free radicals, which disrupt DNA structure and cause cell death. It is selectively toxic to anaerobic protozoa and bacteria due to their unique redox metabolism.
bottom_line: Metronidazole is the treatment for Entamoeba histolytica. It causes the formation of toxic metabolites that damage DNA. Other HY use-cases for metro on USMLE are Giardia, Gardnerella, and Trichomonas. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 74-year-old woman comes to the clinic due to progressive fatigue, nausea, and a persistent “buzzing” in her ears that has been worsening over the past month. She also reports poor appetite and shortness of breath at rest. She denies fever, cough, or vomiting. Her past medical history includes osteoarthritis, for which she takes aspirin and acetaminophen almost daily. She lives alone and reports drinking minimal water. She has no known kidney or lung disease. Vital signs: temperature 36.9°C (98.4°F), blood pressure 134/82 mmHg, heart rate 92/min, respiratory rate 24/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. She appears alert and oriented. There is no hepatosplenomegaly or edema. Laboratory results:  Na⁺                                          134 mEq/L  K⁺                                             4.2 mEq/L  Cl⁻                                            96 mEq/L  HCO₃⁻                                      19 mEq/L  PaCO₂                                      30 mmHg  pH                                             7.37  BUN                                          22 mg/dL  Creatinine                                 1.1 mg/dL Which of the following laboratory patterns is most consistent with this patient's condition?

options.A: Normal acid-base values with isolated elevated BUN
options.B: Normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with respiratory acidosis
options.C: Primary metabolic acidosis with primary respiratory alkalosis
options.D: Primary respiratory acidosis with secondary metabolic compensation
options.E: Primary respiratory alkalosis with compensatory metabolic alkalosis

answer_text: Primary metabolic acidosis with primary respiratory alkalosis
explanation:
Aspirin overdose produces a characteristic two-phase acid-base disturbance. In the first 20 minutes, it directly stimulates the respiratory centers in the brainstem, leading to hyperventilation and an acute respiratory alkalosis. During this phase, the pH is elevated due to excessive CO₂ loss, while bicarbonate remains normal because the kidneys have not yet had time (12–24 hours) to adjust bicarbonate reabsorption or excretion.
This normal bicarbonate level helps confirm the disturbance as acute, distinguishing it from chronic respiratory alkalosis seen in other settings like prolonged high altitude exposure.
After the initial phase, aspirin’s intrinsic acidity (salicylic acid) generates a primary metabolic acidosis by increasing acid load and interfering with cellular metabolism. As a result, patients develop a mixed acid-base disorder, with low pH, low bicarbonate (due to metabolic acidosis), and persistently low CO₂ (from continued hyperventilation). Importantly, this drop in bicarbonate is not a renal compensation for the respiratory
alkalosis, as compensation requires more time to develop. Instead, the bicarbonate falls directly because of aspirin’s acid load, and over time the metabolic acidosis predominates, ultimately lowering the pH despite ongoing respiratory alkalosis.
Aspirin toxicity leads to a high anion gap metabolic acidosis because salicylic acid accumulates in the blood, increasing the concentration of unmeasured anions. When calculating the anion gap using the formula sodium minus the sum of bicarbonate and chloride—Na⁺ − (HCO₃⁻ + Cl⁻)—the value will be elevated, typically above 13, since salicylates are among the classic causes of high anion gap acidosis. This is why
salicylates are the "S" in the mnemonic MUDPILES, which stands for Methanol, Uremia (renal failure), Diabetic ketoacidosis, Phenformin, Iron tablets or Isoniazid, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, and Salicylates. Notably, in salicylate toxicity, sodium and potassium levels are usually normal, which sometimes confuses students if they see arrows on diagrams implying they change. Ultimately, it is the accumulation of acidic
metabolites and the direct acid load from salicylates that create the elevated anion gap and drive the metabolic acidosis component of this mixed acid-base disturbance.
bottom_line: Aspirin causes a mixed respiratory alkalosis-high anion-gap metabolic acidosis. The drop in bicarb following the low CO₂ is not compensation; it just conveniently looks that way.
