These are both primary disturbances because aspirin literally causes both. Pharmacology Subject Biochemistry (General Principles) (Acid/base disorders)

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 56-year-old woman with a history of stage IIIC epithelial ovarian carcinoma presents for evaluation 3 days after receiving a new chemotherapy infusion. She was diagnosed 14 months ago and initially treated with optimal cytoreductive surgery followed by a platinum-based regimen, but her disease progressed after 2 additional lines of chemotherapy. Her oncologist started her on a new agent targeting rapidly dividing cells. She now reports progressive tingling, numbness, and burning pain in her toes and fingertips, which have been gradually ascending over the past 48 hours. She also notes mild weakness when climbing stairs, generalized fatigue, and increased hair shedding. Her past medical history includes controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia. She is a former smoker and has no known history of diabetes or peripheral vascular disease. Medications include lisinopril, atorvastatin, and ondansetron as needed for nausea. She has no known drug allergies. On examination, she is afebrile with stable vital signs. Neurologic exam reveals decreased pinprick and vibration sensation in a stocking-glove distribution, mild weakness in bilateral hip flexors, and slightly reduced ankle reflexes. Upper extremity strength is intact. Her surgical scars are well-healed, and there is no abdominal tenderness or organomegaly. Laboratory evaluation shows normal complete blood count, renal function, and electrolytes. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of this patient’s current chemotherapy drug?

options.A: Disruption of DNA through alkylation of guanine residues

explanation:
Paclitaxel is a microtubule-stabilizing chemotherapeutic agent that binds to β-tubulin and promotes microtubule polymerization while preventing depolymerization. This “freezing” of the mitotic spindle arrests the cell cycle in the M phase (specifically metaphase), blocking proper chromosome segregation and leading to apoptosis of rapidly dividing cells. Unlike agents that inhibit polymerization (eg, vinca alkaloids), paclitaxel
causes excessive stabilization, which prevents the dynamic instability necessary for spindle function.
Paclitaxel is a member of the taxane class, commonly used in the treatment of ovarian, breast, and non–small cell lung cancers, as well as in combination regimens for Kaposi sarcoma. It is particularly important in gynecologic oncology, where it is a mainstay for epithelial ovarian carcinoma. Paclitaxel’s M-phase specificity makes it highly effective against tumors with high mitotic activity, as these cells are most vulnerable to
spindle disruption.
Adverse effects are largely related to damage to rapidly dividing normal cells and interference with microtubule-dependent processes in nondividing cells. The most HY toxicities include myelosuppression (neutropenia), alopecia, and peripheral sensory neuropathy, which results from disruption of axonal microtubules essential for intracellular transport. Neuropathy typically presents in a stocking-glove distribution, as in this
patient. Hypersensitivity reactions can occur due to the drug’s formulation (often containing Cremophor EL), and patients are often premedicated with glucocorticoids and antihistamines.
bottom_line: The patient’s presentation of acute stocking-glove neuropathy, alopecia, and recent chemotherapy for ovarian cancer is most consistent with a taxane, which works by hyperstabilizing polymerized microtubules and arresting cells in M phase. Pharmacology Subject Biochemistry (General Principles) (Cell and molecular biology) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 52-year-old man with chronic liver disease and a recent statin-associated myopathy presents to his primary care physician for lipid management. He has a family history of premature coronary artery disease. Recent labs reveal: Total cholesterol: 260 mg/dL LDL: 185 mg/dL HDL: 42 mg/dL Triglycerides: 135 mg/dL He was previously started on atorvastatin but developed diffuse muscle pain and elevated creatine kinase, prompting discontinuation. The physician starts him on a medication that binds bile acids in the intestine to reduce LDL levels. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism of action of this drug?

options.B: Inhibits cholesterol absorption at brush border
options.C: Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase in the liver
options.D: Inhibits pancreatic lipase in the gut
options.E: Prevents enterohepatic reabsorption of bile acids

answer_text: Prevents enterohepatic reabsorption of bile acids
explanation:
Bile acid sequestrants, such as colesevelam, work by binding bile acids in the intestine and preventing their reabsorption back into the enterohepatic circulation. Normally, bile acids are recycled to the liver, where they suppress the enzyme cholesterol 7α-hydroxylase, which converts cholesterol into bile acids. When sequestrants trap bile acids in the gut and increase their fecal excretion, the liver senses a depletion of bile acids
and upregulates cholesterol 7α-hydroxylase to make more. This process consumes hepatic cholesterol stores, leading the liver to increase expression of LDL receptors on hepatocyte surfaces to pull more LDL cholesterol out of the bloodstream to replenish its cholesterol pool. The net effect is a reduction in circulating LDL cholesterol levels.
bottom_line: Bile acid sequestrants lower LDL by decreasing enterohepatic circulation of bile acids, forcing the liver to make more by converting cholesterol into them, thereby lowering pulling more cholesterol out of the blood and lowering LDL. Pharmacology Subject Biochemistry (General Principles) (Lipid metabolism) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 61-year-old man with hyperlipidemia is prescribed niacin for secondary prevention of cardiovascular disease. At a follow-up visit one week later, he reports that he stopped taking the medication after two doses due to intense facial flushing and warmth, which he describes as “a burning sensation on my face and chest.” He denies rash, shortness of breath, or dizziness. He did not have hives or throat swelling. He says he took the pill in the evening with food, as directed. He has no history of allergies or dermatologic conditions. His current medications include atorvastatin, aspirin, and metoprolol. His blood pressure and heart rate are normal. Physical examination is unremarkable, and there are no signs of angioedema or urticaria. The physician explains that the flushing is a known side effect of niacin, not an allergy, and can often be managed with premedication. The patient is interested in retrying niacin if the side effects can be reduced. The physician recommends taking the next dose with a low-dose nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of this patient’s reaction?
explanation:
Niacin (vitamin B₃) causes cutaneous flushing and warmth, particularly in the face, neck, and upper chest, in a large percentage of patients. This flushing is not an allergic reaction but a direct pharmacologic effect caused by prostaglandin D₂ (PGD₂) and prostaglandin E₂ (PGE₂) release in the skin. Niacin stimulates G-protein–coupled receptors on epidermal Langerhans cells, leading to activation of phospholipase A₂, which
increases arachidonic acid metabolism via the cyclooxygenase (COX) pathway. The resulting prostaglandins dilate cutaneous blood vessels, producing the characteristic redness, warmth, and burning sensation. This effect is dose-dependent and typically attenuates with continued use, but it is often a barrier to adherence early in therapy.
Importantly, the flushing can be blunted or prevented by pretreatment with aspirin or other NSAIDs, which inhibit COX-mediated prostaglandin synthesis. Patients are often advised to take 81–325 mg of aspirin 30 minutes prior to niacin, or to take the dose at bedtime with a low-fat snack to minimize symptoms. Flushing usually begins 20–30 minutes after ingestion and resolves within 1–2 hours. The mechanism is entirely
unrelated to histamine, allergy, or immunologic pathways. Understanding this mechanism helps clinicians differentiate this predictable side effect from more serious reactions such as anaphylaxis or angioedema.
Although newer lipid-lowering agents have largely replaced niacin for many patients, it is still used in certain cases such as severe hypertriglyceridemia or familial dyslipidemias. When used, it is critical to educate patients on this prostaglandin-mediated effect to prevent premature discontinuation. This case highlights how knowledge of pharmacodynamics can directly influence patient education and adherence. Treating the
flushing prophylactically allows patients to remain on therapy longer, increasing the likelihood of lipid control and cardiovascular benefit.
bottom_line: Niacin causes flushing through prostaglandin production, not histamine. This makes sense, since aspirin inhibits prostaglandin synthesis and is used to prevent niacin-induced flushing. Pharmacology Subject Biochemistry (General Principles) (Vitamins, cofactors, nutrients) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 32-year-old woman comes to the clinic because of a 3-day history of discomfort while urinating along with a frequent urge to void small amounts of urine. She also reports mild lower abdominal pressure and states that the symptoms began gradually and have persisted. She denies fever, flank pain, or vaginal discharge. She has no recent travel history, but she has been under increased work stress and has been sleeping poorly. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. She does not have a history of kidney stones. On examination, she appears comfortable, and her vital signs are within normal limits. Abdominal examination shows mild suprapubic tenderness without guarding or rebound. There is no costovertebral angle tenderness. Urinalysis shows pyuria and positive nitrite test. Urine culture grows a lactose-fermenting, gram-negative rod that is reported to be resistant to amoxicillin. The most likely mechanism of antimicrobial resistance exhibited by this organism is which of the following?

options.D: Modification of peptidoglycan termini from D-Ala-D-Ala to D-Ala-D-Lac

explanation:
This patient’s presentation is classic for an uncomplicated lower urinary tract infection (cystitis) caused by Escherichia coli. Symptoms such as dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, and mild suprapubic discomfort, along with a positive nitrite test and pyuria, strongly indicate infection with a gram-negative, nitrate-reducing organism. The urine culture confirming a lactose-fermenting gram-negative rod that is resistant to amoxicillin
makes E coli with β-lactamase production the most likely explanation, as this enzyme can inactivate many β-lactam antibiotics, including aminopenicillins, before they reach their bacterial targets.
β-lactamase enzymes, located in the periplasmic space of gram-negative bacteria, hydrolyze the β-lactam ring of penicillins and many cephalosporins, rendering the antibiotic ineffective. The genes encoding these enzymes are often plasmid mediated and spread via conjugation, facilitating rapid dissemination of resistance traits. Extended-spectrum β-lactamases (ESBLs) can hydrolyze advanced cephalosporins and
aztreonam, which often necessitates carbapenem use. β-lactamase inhibitors (eg, clavulanic acid, tazobactam, sulbactam) are sometimes used in combination therapy, although they are ineffective against all β-lactamase variants, particularly ESBLs and AmpC enzymes.
Escherichia coli is a facultative anaerobic, gram-negative bacillus within the Enterobacteriaceae family. It is a normal intestinal commensal but becomes pathogenic in sterile body sites. It is motile with peritrichous flagella, ferments lactose, and has virulence factors such as P fimbriae for urinary tract colonization. In addition to β-lactamase production, E coli can exhibit other antimicrobial resistance mechanisms, including
reduced expression or alteration of porin proteins (limiting drug entry), overexpression of efflux pumps (removing drugs from the periplasmic space), alterations of antibiotic targets (eg, fluoroquinolone target mutations in DNA gyrase), aminoglycoside-modifying enzymes, and production of carbapenemases. The combination of multiple resistance pathways makes E coli a significant cause of multidrug-resistant infections
worldwide.
Uncomplicated UTIs are common in healthy women due to anatomical predisposition, especially the short urethra and proximity to the perineum. E coli accounts for the majority of cases, followed by Klebsiella pneumoniae and Proteus mirabilis. Diagnosis relies on clinical presentation plus urinalysis findings of bacteriuria and pyuria; nitrite positivity suggests Enterobacteriaceae. Management typically involves short courses of
trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, nitrofurantoin, or fosfomycin, with empirical therapy adjusted based on local resistance data.
bottom_line: Uncomplicated UTIs are most often caused by E coli, which commonly develops β-lactam resistance via plasmid-mediated β-lactamase production. These enzymes hydrolyze the β-lactam ring, preventing antibiotic binding to PBPs and inhibiting cell wall synthesis disruption. Pharmacology Subject Microbiology (General Principles) (Bacteriology) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with a 1-week history of vaginal itching and increased discharge. She describes the discharge as frothy and foul-smelling. She also reports mild dysuria and dyspareunia. Her last menstrual period was two weeks ago, and she is sexually active with multiple male partners. She does not use barrier contraception. On pelvic examination, there is vulvar erythema and a frothy greenish-yellow vaginal discharge. The cervix appears inflamed with punctate hemorrhages. Vaginal pH is 6.0. Microscopy of a saline wet mount reveals motile flagellated organisms. There are numerous neutrophils on the slide. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?
explanation:
Trichomonas vaginalis is a flagellated protozoan and the only protozoal cause of sexually transmitted vaginitis.
Classic features of trichomoniasis include profuse, malodorous, frothy, yellow-green vaginal discharge, vulvovaginal inflammation, and a “strawberry cervix” due to capillary dilation and punctate hemorrhage on the ectocervix. The diagnosis is typically made by direct visualization of motile trichomonads on a saline wet mount. The vaginal pH is usually elevated (>4.5), and numerous neutrophils may be observed due to the
inflammatory response. The standard treatment is a single dose of oral metronidazole, and both the patient and her sexual partners must be treated to prevent reinfection.
Unlike bacterial vaginosis, trichomoniasis presents with inflammatory symptoms such as pruritus, burning, and visible mucosal irritation. Wet mount microscopy is the most rapid and inexpensive diagnostic tool, but nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) is more sensitive when needed. T. vaginalis is transmitted exclusively through sexual contact and does not form cysts, relying on direct transfer of trophozoites. If untreated, it
can increase the risk of HIV acquisition and adverse pregnancy outcomes such as preterm delivery.
Condoms help reduce transmission, but recurrence is common in untreated partners.
bottom_line: Trichomonas vaginalis causes vaginitis with frothy, malodorous, yellow-green discharge, strawberry cervix, and motile flagellated organisms on wet mount. Diagnosis is clinical and microscopic, and treatment requires oral metronidazole with simultaneous partner therapy. Pharmacology Subject Microbiology (General Principles) (Parasitology) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 36-year-old man with HIV comes to the emergency department with a 2-week history of headache, fever, and progressive disorientation. He has had several episodes of vomiting and reports his neck is stiff. CSF analysis shows elevated opening pressure, high protein, low glucose, and high lymphocytes. Which of the following is the mechanism of action of the most appropriate initial pharmacologic therapy for this patient's illness?

options.A: Binds ergosterol and forms membrane pores B. Blocks conversion of squalene to lanosterol C. Inhibits β-1,3-glucan synthesis in fungal cell wall D. Inhibits fungal DNA and RNA synthesis E. Inhibits fungal lanosterol 14α-demethylase

answer_text: Binds ergosterol and forms membrane pores B. Blocks conversion of squalene to lanosterol C. Inhibits β-1,3-glucan synthesis in fungal cell wall D. Inhibits fungal DNA and RNA synthesis E. Inhibits fungal lanosterol 14α-demethylase
explanation:
"Binds ergosterol and forms membrane pores" refers to amphotericin B. Ergosterol is the fungal cell equivalent of cholesterol. Regarding its synthesis, we have squalene --> lanosterol --> ergosterol.
Cryptococcus neoformans is the HY cause of fungal meningitis, classically in immunocompromised patients. It demonstrates narrow-based budding (Neoformans = Narrow) and is diagnosed using India ink prep, where everything stains black except for the encapsulated yeast, which appear as bright white halos.
We treat with amphotericin B + flucytosine, followed by a long fluconazole taper. But if USMLE forces you to choose, they want amphotericin B as the singular HY treatment. We use this agent as a last resort for CNS and/or disseminated fungal infections since it is very powerful and can cause hypersensitivity reactions and electrolyte disturbances.
Mucicarmine staining can also be used, which is a red/pink stain. USMLE loves to show this image as well. Latex agglutination, however, is actually the most accurate test overall. It just happens to be that India ink prep and mucicarmine stain images are buzzy and HY.
Fungal meningitis presents with CSF analysis of low glucose, high protein, and high lymphocytes. Opening pressure can be increased in fungal meningitis, but this is non-specific and can be increased in other infections too.
It should be noted that nystatin also pokes holes in the ergosterol cell membrane, but the use case is very different. It is used topically for vulvovaginal candidiasis and as mouthwash for oropharyngeal candidiasis (don't confuse with candidal esophagitis, which we use fluconazole for instead).
bottom_line: Avascular necrosis (AVN; aka osteonecrosis), is the most likely diagnosis in this patient with insidious-onset monoarticular hip pain and a history of immunosuppressive therapy following renal transplantation. USMLE wants you to know that corticosteroids, which are commonly used post-transplant, are one of the most well-established risk factors for AVN. Corticosteroids contribute to avascular necrosis of the hip by increasing fat cell size and number within the bone marrow, which raises intraosseous pressure and compresses small blood vessels. This impairs blood flow to the femoral head, leading to localized ischemia and bone death. Over time, the structural integrity of the femoral head collapses due to impaired remodeling and repair. AVN often presents with deep, poorly localized pain in the groin or buttock that worsens with activity and eventually becomes present at rest. Early in the disease course, physical exams may reveal restricted range of motion, particularly with internal rotation, and x-rays may be normal, necessitating MRI for definitive diagnosis. The hip is the most commonly affected site, and the absence of systemic symptoms, fever, or joint swelling helps distinguish AVN from inflammatory or infectious causes. X-ray of the hip can show a contracted / collapsed femoral head.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 56-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with progressive right-sided hip pain over the past 6 weeks. She describes the pain as deep and aching, worse with weight-bearing, and present even at rest. She denies any history of trauma, fever, or recent infections. The patient underwent a kidney transplant one year ago and takes multiple medications daily. Physical examination reveals pain with internal rotation and limited range of motion of the right hip. There is no joint swelling, erythema, or warmth. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers and white blood cell count. X-ray shows a contracted femoral head. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 13-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her mother due to intermittent lower abdominal discomfort and a feeling of "fullness" for the past 4 days. She has not yet started menstruating. Her appetite and bowel habits are normal, and she denies fever, nausea, or urinary symptoms. She has no prior medical or surgical history. On physical examination, her abdomen is soft with no tenderness or distention. Pelvic examination reveals a firm, non-tender right adnexal mass. Ultrasonography shows an 8-cm right ovarian mass with solid and cystic components and areas of calcification. There is no ascites or evidence of metastatic disease. Laboratory studies, including beta-hCG and inflammatory markers, are within normal limits. Surgical consultation is obtained for elective removal. Histologic analysis of the mass is most likely to reveal which of the following?

options.A: Benign cyst composed of ovarian follicles arrested in development
options.B: Benign tumor of the ovary derived from all three germ layers
options.C: Malignant epithelial tumor of the ovary with serous architecture
options.D: Mass containing trophoblastic proliferation and hydropic villi
options.E: Periappendiceal inflammatory mass composed of pus and necrotic tissue

answer_text: Benign tumor of the ovary derived from all three germ layers
explanation:
The most likely diagnosis is a mature teratoma (dermoid cyst), a common benign germ cell tumor seen in girls and young women, especially in the first two decades of life.
These tumors are characterized by tissue elements from all three germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, and endoderm), which can include skin, teeth, hair, bone, neural tissue, and even respiratory or gastrointestinal epithelium. On imaging, mature teratomas often appear as complex adnexal masses with both cystic and solid components, sometimes with echogenic nodules or calcifications due to fat or bone content. These
masses are often asymptomatic but can present with abdominal pain, pressure, or fullness, especially if they grow large or cause ovarian torsion. Surgical removal is typically curative, and the tumor carries an excellent prognosis when benign features are confirmed histologically.
The pathogenesis involves aberrant differentiation of totipotent germ cells, which normally migrate to the genital ridge during embryogenesis. These germ cells can give rise to a variety of mature tissues in a disorganized pattern, leading to the teratomatous architecture. Because mature teratomas are hormonally inactive and slow-growing, patients usually present due to size-related discomfort or incidental imaging findings. The
absence of ascites, systemic symptoms, or elevated tumor markers also supports a benign diagnosis.
In this patient, the prepubertal status, absence of systemic illness, and ultrasonographic findings of a complex ovarian mass with calcification strongly favor a mature cystic teratoma.
bottom_line: Mature teratomas are benign ovarian germ cell tumors containing tissues from all three germ layers, commonly found in adolescent girls. Their complex imaging features and favorable prognosis distinguish them from malignant or infectious pelvic masses. Pharmacology Subject Pathology (General Principles) (Neoplasia) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 22-year-old man undergoes a routine orthopedic procedure for a tibial fracture. The operation proceeds uneventfully until about 45 minutes after induction, when the anesthesiologist notes rising end-tidal carbon dioxide despite increased ventilation. Soon afterward, the patient develops generalized muscle rigidity, a rapid increase in core body temperature, tachycardia, and dark urine. He had been otherwise healthy with no chronic illnesses, and his pre-operative evaluation was normal. His family history is notable for a cousin who died suddenly following surgery, though the details were unclear. He does not smoke or drink alcohol, and takes no regular medications. Laboratory studies obtained intraoperatively show metabolic acidosis with elevated potassium and creatine kinase levels. Which of the following best explains this patient’s condition?

options.A: Autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction
options.B: Defective calcium reuptake by sarcoplasmic Ca²⁺-ATPase pumps
options.C: Genetic defect in the skeletal muscle ryanodine receptor
options.D: Impaired binding of tropomyosin to the thin filament
options.E: Mutations in mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation enzymes

answer_text: Genetic defect in the skeletal muscle ryanodine receptor
explanation:
This intraoperative crisis with rapidly rising end-tidal CO₂ despite hyperventilation, generalized rigidity, hyperthermia, tachycardia, hyperkalemia, and marked CK elevation is classic for malignant hyperthermia precipitated by volatile anesthetics and/or succinylcholine in a genetically susceptible host. The core defect is an abnormal sarcoplasmic reticulum calcium-release channel in skeletal muscle encoded by RYR1, which
opens excessively when exposed to triggering agents, flooding the myoplasm with Ca²⁺ and producing fulminant, sustained contraction and explosive ATP consumption. Consequences include heat generation, lactic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis with myoglobinuria (dark urine), and life-threatening arrhythmias.
Malignant hyperthermia is usually inherited in an autosomal dominant pattern with variable penetrance, often with a suggestive family history of perioperative deaths or “fever under anesthesia.” Triggers include halogenated inhalational anesthetics (eg, sevoflurane, desflurane, isoflurane) and the depolarizing neuromuscular blocker succinylcholine. The hypermetabolic state appears minutes to hours after exposure, with the
earliest, most sensitive intraoperative sign being a sudden ETCO₂ rise unresponsive to increased ventilation; masseter spasm after succinylcholine can be an early clue.
Immediate management requires discontinuation of all triggering agents, 100% oxygen with aggressive hyperventilation, active external and internal cooling, large-volume IV fluids, and correction of acidosis and hyperkalemia. Dantrolene is the specific therapy; it binds the defective channel and reduces Ca²⁺ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, breaking the contraction–hypermetabolism cycle and decreasing heat
production and rhabdomyolysis. Avoid calcium channel blockers with dantrolene due to risk of hyperkalemia and cardiovascular collapse. After stabilization, patients need ICU monitoring for recrudescence, renal protection from myoglobin, and counseling/testing of relatives.
Definitive confirmation can be obtained with the caffeine–halothane contracture test on a muscle biopsy or by molecular testing for pathogenic variants in RYR1. Prevention hinges on careful preoperative screening, nontriggering anesthetic plans (eg, total IV anesthesia with propofol, nondepolarizing NMBs), and immediate availability of dantrolene in operative suites.
bottom_line: Malignant hyperthermia is a life-threatening hypermetabolic crisis triggered by anesthetics in patients with RYR1 mutations, causing uncontrolled Ca²⁺ release, muscle rigidity, acidosis, and rhabdomyolysis. Prompt recognition and dantrolene administration are lifesaving. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Anesthesia) System

subject: Pharmacology
topic: Methimazole causes agranulocytosis. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System
stem: A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 2-month history of palpitations, unintentional weight loss, heat intolerance, and frequent bowel movements. He also reports increased anxiety and difficulty sleeping. He denies chest pain, drug use, or recent travel. Vital signs are: temperature 37.3°C (99.1°F), blood pressure 124/72 mm Hg, pulse 102/min, and respiratory rate 18/min. Physical exam reveals moist skin, fine tremor of the hands, and a diffusely enlarged, non-tender thyroid. There is no thyroid nodule, bruit, or ophthalmopathy. Thyroid function tests reveal a suppressed TSH and elevated free T4. The patient is started on methimazole therapy. Which of the following is the most feared complication of this patient’s treatment?
explanation:
Methimazole is an antithyroid thionamide that inhibits thyroid peroxidase, preventing the synthesis of thyroid hormones. While effective, its most feared complication is agranulocytosis, a profound neutropenia that can lead to life-threatening infections. It typically presents within the first 3 months of therapy with signs such mouth ulcers, sore throat, and fever.
Methimazole should be immediately discontinued when agranulocytosis is suspected, and the patient should undergo complete blood count with differential to assess the absolute neutrophil count. Routine monitoring is not recommended, but patients must be advised to report infection symptoms promptly.
Other common methimazole side effects include rash, minor leukopenia, and cholestatic liver injury. Agranulocytosis, however, is the most serious and unpredictable. If it develops, methimazole must be stopped permanently, and alternative treatments (e.g., radioactive iodine or surgery) should be considered. This adverse effect is idiosyncratic, not dose-dependent, and requires high clinical suspicion for timely diagnosis.  Other
drugs that can cause febrile neutropenia include clozapine, propylthiouracil, sulfasalazine, TMP-SMX, and methotrexate.
bottom_line: Methimazole is an antithyroid thionamide that inhibits thyroid peroxidase, preventing the synthesis of thyroid hormones.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents due to progressive lethargy, poor feeding, and vomiting over the past two days. He has a history of generalized epilepsy and was recently started on a new antiepileptic medication. He has no prior liver disease, and his only medication is the new antiepileptic. He has not had recent travel or sick contacts. On exam, he appears fatigued but responsive. Temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure 96/62 mm Hg, pulse 98/min, and respiratory rate 20/min. Sclera are anicteric, and the abdomen is soft without hepatomegaly. Neurologic exam shows no focal deficits. Labs reveal elevated liver transaminases and mildly increased serum ammonia. Serum glucose and electrolytes are within normal limits. Which of the following best explains the mechanism underlying this patient’s symptoms?

options.A: Blockade of T-type calcium channels in the thalamus

explanation:
Valproic acid is an anticonvulsant and mood stabilizer that can cause several important adverse effects due to its metabolic and pharmacologic properties. It is associated with dose-dependent hepatotoxicity, particularly in young children, because it is metabolized extensively by the liver and can lead to the accumulation of toxic metabolites that cause microvesicular steatosis and hepatic failure.
Valproic acid can also produce a fine postural tremor as a dose-related neurologic side effect due to enhanced GABAergic activity in the central nervous system.
Importantly, it increases the risk of neural tube defects in the fetus, such as spina bifida, because it interferes with folate metabolism and reduces folate absorption and bioavailability during early embryonic development.
This Q is not about memorizing mitochondrial beta-oxidation as though that's special. It's the fact that the vignette focuses on hepatotoxicity and choice C is the only one listed that singles out the liver. USMLE will do this sometimes, where there's 5 obscure answers you're not sure about, but one just seems to relate more. Tangentially though, it is HY you know that for Reye syndrome caused by aspirin use in kids, impairment
of mitochondrial beta-oxidation is actually a specific mechanism they want you to know.
bottom_line: Valproic acid can cause hepatotoxicity, tremor, and neural tube defects. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 30-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department due to agitation, fever, and diarrhea. She was recently started on fluoxetine for depression and began taking an over-the-counter supplement recommended by a friend. Her husband believes it was St. John’s wort. On exam, she is diaphoretic and flushed, with a temperature of 105.2°F (40.7°C), blood pressure of 160/90 mm Hg, and heart rate of 130/min. She has hyperreflexia, tremor, and myoclonus in her lower limbs. There are no murmurs or signs of endocarditis. What is the most appropriate next step in diagnosis?
explanation:
This patient is presenting with serotonin syndrome, a life-threatening condition caused by excess serotonergic activity in the CNS and peripheral nervous system. It is most often triggered by drug-drug interactions such as SSRI + St. John’s wort, SSRI + MAOI, or with serotonergic agents like trazodone or lithium. The trigger in this case is the combination of fluoxetine (an SSRI) and St. John’s wort, a known serotonergic herbal
supplement.
The presentation is classic: acute onset of hyperthermia (105.2°F), diaphoresis, diarrhea, tachycardia, and neuromuscular findings such as myoclonus, tremor, and hyperreflexia—especially in the lower limbs. These findings, particularly the severe fever and neuromuscular excitability, distinguish serotonin syndrome from other causes of flushing and diarrhea.
The most appropriate diagnostic test is urinary 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA), a serotonin metabolite that will be elevated in serotonin syndrome. It’s important to note that 5-HIAA is also elevated in carcinoid syndrome, but that condition is not drug-induced, progresses chronically, and is associated with right-sided valvular disease (e.g., tricuspid regurgitation)—features that are absent here.
Once diagnosed, serotonin syndrome is managed by discontinuing the offending agents and administering cyproheptadine, a serotonin receptor antagonist.
bottom_line: Serotonin syndrome is a life-threatening toxidrome triggered by serotonergic drug combinations (e.g., SSRI + St. John’s wort, MAOI, trazodone, or lithium). Presents with fever, flushing, hyperreflexia, tremor, diarrhea, and myoclonus, not seen in carcinoid syndrome. Urinary 5-HIAA is elevated and used for diagnosis; cyproheptadine is the treatment of choice. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 29-year-old man presents to the outpatient clinic with new-onset ankle swelling and fatigue. He recently completed a 2-week course of allopurinol for management of hyperuricemia. He denies rash, fever, dysuria, urgency, or flank pain. Vitals are within normal limits. On physical exam, he has bilateral pitting edema of the lower extremities. Labs show a creatinine of 2.3 mg/dL and BUN of 34 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows mild proteinuria and hematuria, but no RBC casts, no bacteria, and no nitrites or leukocyte esterase. Urine culture is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
This patient has acute kidney injury with new-onset peripheral edema, following recent allopurinol use for gout. The urinalysis shows mild proteinuria and hematuria, with no bacteria or casts, which strongly suggests allergic interstitial nephritis (AIN), also known as interstitial nephropathy or tubulointerstitial nephritis.
AIN is best thought of as a hypersensitivity reaction in the kidney — most often caused by NSAIDs, β-lactams, cephalosporins, or allopurinol. The classic textbook/pass-level presentation includes maculopapular rash and urinary WBCs (sometimes eosinophils), though only about half of cases mention the rash, and the eosinophiluria may be omitted entirely in the vignette. In other words, some questions may only show mild
proteinuria or hematuria with no rash or WBCs, and even that still points toward interstitial nephritis if the timeline and drug match.
Occasionally, AIN may present with isolated edema from NSAID use, mimicking a pre-renal picture. The injury in AIN affects the renal tubule, not the glomerulus or vasculature — another tested point.
bottom_line: Interstitial nephritis is the answer on USMLE for a patient with eosinophiluria + rash in the setting of NSAID, β-lactam, cephalosporin, or allopurinol use. Some Qs just mention mild proteinuria + hematuria and don't mention the WBCs/eosinophils in the urine at all. So just be aware of this alternate presentation. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 20-year-old college student comes to the clinic due to increased thirst and frequent urination over the past month. She reports waking multiple times at night to urinate and has been carrying a water bottle with her everywhere due to persistent dry mouth. She has also noticed muscle weakness and recent difficulty concentrating during lectures. Her appetite has decreased slightly, and she has unintentionally lost 3 kg over the past few weeks. She has no history of diabetes or renal disease. Past medical history is unremarkable, and she takes no regular medications. However, she admits to recently using a leftover antibiotic found in her family’s medicine cabinet for what she thought was a “sinus infection.” She says the medication was “a few years old” and yellowed in color. Physical examination is notable for dry mucous membranes and mild orthostatic hypotension. Neurologic exam shows diminished vibratory sense in the lower extremities. Which of the following best describes the expected pattern of reabsorption in this patient’s proximal tubules?

options.E: ↑ Amino acids, ↑ Bicarbonate, ↓ Glucose, ↑ Phosphate Submit

explanation:
This patient is presenting with signs and symptoms of Fanconi syndrome, a disorder of the proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) resulting in defective reabsorption of several key solutes. Fanconi syndrome leads to urinary loss of glucose, amino acids, phosphate, and bicarbonate, despite normal or low serum levels. The proximal tubule normally reabsorbs nearly all filtered glucose and amino acids, most phosphate, and a large
portion of bicarbonate.
When this function is impaired, patients develop glucosuria, aminoaciduria, phosphaturia, and bicarbonaturia, leading to corresponding systemic abnormalities such as volume depletion, muscle weakness, and non–anion gap metabolic acidosis (from bicarbonate loss, i.e., type II renal tubular acidosis).
One classic and tested cause of Fanconi syndrome is expired tetracycline, particularly formulations that degrade into nephrotoxic compounds over time. Other causes include certain chemotherapeutic agents (e.g., ifosfamide), heavy metals, Wilson disease, and inherited conditions like cystinosis. Expired tetracyclines can accumulate degradation products that directly injure the PCT epithelium, disrupting active transport
mechanisms for multiple solutes.
The presence of glucosuria in a patient with normal serum glucose is a particularly high-yield clue for PCT dysfunction. Likewise, phosphate and bicarbonate losses can contribute to muscle symptoms and acid-base disturbances. Unlike diabetic glycosuria, Fanconi-related glycosuria occurs even in the absence of hyperglycemia.
This patient’s polyuria is the result of osmotic diuresis caused by excessive solute loss in the urine. In contrast to central or nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, where free water loss is the primary issue, Fanconi syndrome leads to a solute-driven diuresis. The muscle weakness and weight loss may reflect phosphate wasting and chronic acidosis. Diminished vibratory sensation can be related to electrolyte abnormalities or the
overall metabolic disturbance.
The key diagnostic feature is the simultaneous downregulation of multiple PCT transporters, resulting in decreased reabsorption of glucose, amino acids, phosphate, and bicarbonate—captured correctly in answer choice B.
bottom_line: Fanconi syndrome results in decreased proximal tubular reabsorption of amino acids, glucose, phosphate, and bicarbonate. Expired tetracyclines are a classic cause and can lead to osmotic diuresis, electrolyte loss, and type II renal tubular acidosis. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 32-year-old woman, gravida 2 para 1, presents for a routine fetal anatomy scan at 20 weeks gestation. She has a history of Graves disease and has been on antithyroid therapy for the past 3 years. At the time of confirming her pregnancy, she was still taking methimazole, which she had been using consistently for several months. Her medication was later switched to propylthiouracil at her first prenatal visit, which occurred around 12 weeks gestation. She reports no other medication use, no infections, and no substance use. Her first pregnancy was uncomplicated. The ultrasound reveals a live, singleton fetus with normal growth parameters and normal fluid volume. The maternal exam is unremarkable. She asks if her early pregnancy medication could have affected the baby. Which of the following fetal anomalies is most likely associated with this patient’s medication exposure?
explanation:
This patient has a known diagnosis of Graves disease and continued methimazole through the first trimester — the critical period of organogenesis. Although she later transitioned to PTU, the teratogenic window of methimazole exposure had already occurred. Methimazole is known to cross the placenta and has been linked to congenital anomalies when administered during the first trimester.
The most classic and USMLE-tested anomaly associated with methimazole is aplasia cutis, a localized congenital absence of skin, most often affecting the scalp. Although the ultrasound does not detail the specific abnormality, the timeline and medication history are sufficient to anticipate this risk. The mechanism of methimazole teratogenicity is not fully understood but is believed to involve disruption of embryonic skin and
epithelial development, especially when exposure occurs during weeks 6–10. In addition to aplasia cutis, other defects associated with methimazole include choanal atresia and esophageal atresia, but these are less commonly tested.
The important USMLE takeaway is the timing-dependent risk: methimazole is teratogenic in the first trimester and avoided for that reason. PTU, while hepatotoxic with long-term use, is the safer antithyroid agent during the early stages of pregnancy. The decision to start PTU after 12–13 weeks reflects standard practice, as organogenesis is largely complete by then. However, in this case, methimazole exposure has already
occurred during a vulnerable developmental window. Despite a normal anatomy scan overall, the fetus remains at increased risk for certain defects due to early drug exposure.
bottom_line: Methimazole is teratogenic in the first trimester due to its effect on developing epithelial tissues. The most classic USMLE-associated defect is aplasia cutis, especially when methimazole is used during organogenesis. PTU is preferred in the first trimester, with methimazole reserved for later pregnancy to avoid hepatotoxicity. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 35-year-old man with a history of major depressive disorder and generalized anxiety disorder is brought to the emergency department by his partner due to confusion, restlessness, and tremors. Two weeks ago, he visited his psychiatrist complaining of persistent fatigue and lack of motivation. In response, his dose of sertraline was increased from 50 mg to 100 mg daily, and he was started on an as-needed prescription for sumatriptan for migraine headaches. Three days ago, he also began taking an over-the-counter herbal supplement containing St. John's wort that was recommended by a friend. Since yesterday evening, he has become increasingly agitated, has had difficulty sleeping, and complained of feeling hot and sweaty. Earlier today, he developed muscle twitching in his legs and began speaking incoherently, prompting his partner to seek emergency care. Upon arrival, he is awake but appears anxious and diaphoretic. He is unable to follow complex commands and repeatedly asks the same questions. He is noted to have tremulous hands and continuous lower extremity clonus. Vital signs are: temperature 39.1°C (102.4°F), heart rate 122/min, blood pressure 148/90 mm Hg, respiratory rate 22/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Neurologic examination shows hyperreflexia in both upper and lower extremities and inducible ankle clonus bilaterally. There is no nuchal rigidity, papilledema, or focal weakness. Skin is flushed but without rash. The remainder of the physical exam is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's presentation?
explanation:
This patient presents with acute onset of altered mental status, hyperthermia, autonomic instability, and neuromuscular hyperactivity (hyperreflexia, clonus, tremor), consistent with serotonin syndrome. The history of serotonergic medication use (sertraline) in combination with other serotonergic agents — notably sumatriptan and St. John's wort — further supports this diagnosis. Serotonin syndrome typically results from excess
central and peripheral serotonin activity and most often occurs within 24 hours of medication changes, dose increases, or polypharmacy involving serotonergic drugs. The triad of mental status changes (anxiety, confusion), autonomic dysregulation (fever, hypertension, diaphoresis), and neuromuscular excitation (hyperreflexia, clonus) is characteristic.
Serotonergic toxicity involves overstimulation of 5-HT1A and 5-HT2A receptors, particularly in the CNS. Neuromuscular findings often predominate in the lower extremities and may include tremor, inducible or spontaneous clonus, and increased muscle tone. Reflexes are typically brisk. In contrast to neuroleptic malignant syndrome (which evolves over days and features “lead-pipe” rigidity and hyporeflexia), serotonin syndrome
is abrupt in onset and involves hyperreflexia. Management includes discontinuation of all serotonergic agents and supportive care; in severe cases, serotonin antagonists such as cyproheptadine may be used. Recognition is critical, as progression to seizures, rhabdomyolysis, or cardiovascular collapse can occur if untreated.
bottom_line: Serotonin syndrome presents with the triad of altered mental status, autonomic instability, and neuromuscular hyperactivity (e.g., clonus, hyperreflexia). It is most often triggered by polypharmacy involving serotonergic agents such as SSRIs, MAOIs, triptans, or herbal supplements like St. John’s wort. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 24-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by emergency medical services after she was found unresponsive on the grass at an outdoor music festival. According to her friends, she had been dancing and acting normally earlier in the day. They last saw her about 45 minutes prior to collapse and assumed she had gone to get food or water. When they found her, she was lying on her side and difficult to arouse. They deny seeing her consume alcohol but recall that she took several pills given to her by another attendee. They are unaware of her taking any prescription medications and say she has no known medical history. She recently graduated from college and works as a part-time yoga instructor. There is no known history of psychiatric illness, and she has never expressed suicidal ideation. Her family history is non-contributory. She does not smoke or drink regularly but has used recreational substances “occasionally” in the past, including marijuana and ecstasy. On arrival at the ED, the patient is unresponsive to sternal rub and moans only briefly to painful stimuli. She does not follow commands or open her eyes spontaneously. Her vital signs are notable for a pulse of 52/min, respiratory rate of 7/min, blood pressure of 108/64 mm Hg, oxygen saturation of 89% on room air, and temperature of 36.8°C (98.2°F). Pupils are 1 mm bilaterally and reactive to light. Skin is warm and dry, and there is no flushing, rash, cyanosis, or signs of trauma. There is no scleral icterus or needle marks. Neurologic exam shows decreased spontaneous movement but preserved deep tendon reflexes. There is no nystagmus. ECG shows sinus bradycardia with a normal QRS and QT interval. A small pouch containing unlabeled tablets is found in her backpack. The emergency medicine team prepares to intubate for airway protection. Which of the following substances is the most likely cause of her presentation?
explanation:
This patient presents with the classic triad of opioid overdose: central nervous system depression, miosis (pinpoint pupils), and respiratory depression. The most dangerous feature is the marked hypoventilation (RR 7/min) with resultant hypoxia, which can lead to respiratory arrest and death if untreated. Opioids like heroin, oxycodone, hydromorphone, fentanyl, and morphine bind to μ-opioid receptors in the brainstem,
suppressing the respiratory centers’ response to carbon dioxide and reducing the drive to breathe. Her bradycardia and somnolence are also typical. This clinical picture is further supported by the rapid progression after ingestion of unknown pills in a social setting.
Naloxone is the antidote for opioid overdose and acts as a competitive antagonist at opioid receptors, reversing both the sedation and respiratory depression. It has a rapid onset of action, but a short half-life, which means repeat dosing or a continuous infusion may be necessary, particularly for longer-acting opioids. If administered promptly, naloxone can dramatically improve respiratory effort and mental status. Supportive
care with oxygen and airway protection may also be required. In this case, immediate naloxone administration is indicated, even prior to further diagnostic testing.
bottom_line: Opioid overdose classically presents with CNS depression, bradypnea, bradycardia, and miosis. Respiratory depression is the most dangerous feature and the leading cause of death. Naloxone is the immediate treatment of choice and can rapidly reverse symptoms. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 32-year-old woman at 30 weeks gestation is brought to the emergency department with sudden-onset chest pain radiating to her back, shortness of breath, and lightheadedness. She has a history of recurrent pericarditis and admits to using “a recreational stimulant” at a party the night before. On examination, she is diaphoretic, hypertensive, and tachypneic. BP is 180/110 mm Hg, pulse is 112/min, and a high-pitched early diastolic murmur is heard at the left sternal border. Fetal monitoring reveals decreased placental perfusion, and she becomes increasingly somnolent. Transesophageal echocardiography shows a Stanford type A aortic dissection with aortic regurgitation. Which of the following substances is the most likely cause of her symptoms?
explanation:
Cocaine is a potent sympathomimetic that blocks the reuptake of various neurotramsmitters, such norepinephrine, dopamine, and serotonin, leading to intense vasoconstriction, hypertension, tachycardia, and increased myocardial oxygen demand.
These effects predispose to vasoconstrictive effects within the brain, as well as at the placenta, leading to intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR) and sometimes placental abruption.
Aortic dissection can occur due to hypertension, which can retrograde propagate toward the aortic root causing aortic root dilatation and aortic regurg. This is why we have a diastolic murmur here. Cocaine is also linked to serous pericarditis and myocardial ischemia.
Treatment is benzos for sympathetic overactivity and agitation, with observation in the emergency room.
bottom_line: Cocaine prevents the reuptake of norepinephrine, leading to hypertension, aortic dissection, and concomitant aortic regurg. Serous pericarditis can also be seen. USMLE can mention reduced cerebral or placental blood flow, with the latter causing IUGR and placental abruption. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 42-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden shortness of breath and discomfort in her chest that worsens with breathing. She returned from an international flight two days ago and has been using oral contraceptive pills. Her medical history is unremarkable. On examination, her temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), pulse is 112/min, blood pressure is 108/72 mmHg, respiratory rate is 24/min, and oxygen saturation is 93% on room air. She appears mildly anxious and is breathing rapidly. Cardiopulmonary examination is normal. An ECG shows sinus tachycardia. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
In a patient with high clinical suspicion for pulmonary embolism (PE), empiric anticoagulation should be initiated before confirmatory imaging if the patient is hemodynamically stable and the risk of bleeding is low. Administering anticoagulation early helps prevent clot propagation and decreases mortality. Unfractionated heparin is preferred when rapid reversal may be needed, while low molecular weight heparin is suitable for
many stable patients.
This patient’s acute dyspnea, tachycardia, estrogen use, and recent immobility after a long flight place her at high risk for PE. When the clinical probability is high, delaying treatment for imaging or laboratory testing can be harmful. Initiating anticoagulation immediately is the safest and most evidence-based step. The diagnostic pathway for PE depends on the Wells score, a validated clinical prediction rule that assesses pretest
probability of PE.
The Wells criteria include the following factors: clinical signs of deep vein thrombosis, PE as the most likely diagnosis, tachycardia, recent immobilization or surgery, previous DVT or PE, hemoptysis, and malignancy. Each of these elements contributes a specific number of points, and the total score stratifies patients into low, moderate, or high probability for PE. A score greater than 6 suggests high risk, between 2 and 6
indicates moderate risk, and less than 2 indicates low risk. In high-probability cases, empiric anticoagulation is recommended while awaiting confirmatory imaging.
bottom_line: For patients with high pretest probability of PE, anticoagulation should be initiated immediately before diagnostic confirmation. The Wells score estimates this probability using clinical signs and risk factors. Imaging and D-dimer use depend on that risk stratification, but treatment should not be delayed when risk is high. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 60-year-old man presents to clinic for follow-up after three gout attacks in the past year, including a recent episode affecting his right knee that was treated with oral corticosteroids. He reports complete symptom resolution and currently has no joint pain or swelling. Past medical history includes obesity, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease stage 2. He takes hydrochlorothiazide, lisinopril, and amlodipine. Laboratory testing reveals a serum uric acid level of 9.8 mg/dL. He does not consume alcohol or sugary beverages. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
This patient meets indications for chronic urate-lowering therapy: recurrent attacks (≥2/year) and serum uric acid >6.8 mg/dL.
Allopurinol, a xanthine oxidase inhibitor, reduces uric acid production and is first-line for preventing future gout flares. It should be started only after the acute episode has resolved to avoid provoking another flare. Though currently asymptomatic, patients with recurrent gout are at risk for tophaceous disease, joint destruction, and uric acid nephrolithiasis. Initiation of allopurinol now will reduce long-term complications and help
maintain uric acid below the target level of 6 mg/dL.
Colchicine may be co-administered as prophylaxis when starting urate-lowering therapy, but it does not lower serum urate levels itself. Diuretics like hydrochlorothiazide should be reviewed as they increase uric acid levels and may need to be substituted with alternatives such as losartan. Lifestyle modification should be reinforced, but pharmacologic intervention is essential given recurrence.
Early intervention is key to preventing irreversible joint damage. The decision to start urate-lowering therapy is a common test point on Step 2 CK.
bottom_line: Allopurinol is the first-line agent for chronic urate-lowering therapy in patients with recurrent gout and elevated serum urate. It should be initiated after resolution of acute flares to prevent long-term complications like joint destruction and tophi formation. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 26-year-old sexually active woman presents to the clinic with a 4-day history of worsening joint pain and a new rash. She describes the joint pain as migrating, initially affecting her wrists and now her right knee and left ankle. She also reports low-grade fever and mild fatigue but denies vaginal discharge or abdominal pain. Two days prior, she noticed small, painless pustules on her fingers and shins. Her last menstrual period was 3 days ago. She has no history of autoimmune disease and is not taking any medications. On examination, there is tenderness over the flexor tendons of both hands, moderate swelling of the right knee, and several scattered pustular lesions on her extremities. Synovial fluid analysis shows 15,000 leukocytes/μL and no crystals. Which of the following treatments is most likely to be effective?
explanation:
This patient has disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI), a complication of Neisseria gonorrhoeae bacteremia. DGI often occurs in menstruating women and presents with the triad of 1) arthritis, 2) tenosynovitis (inflammation of tendon sheaths, usually presenting as deQuervain tenosynovitis at the lateral wrist), and 3) cutaneous papules/pustules.
Ceftriaxone, a third-generation cephalosporin, is the treatment of choice for N. gonorrhoeae. All patients must be co-treated for Chlamydia trachomatis unless it has been ruled out, using oral doxycycline (or azithromycin in pregnancy).
When treating for gonococcus, patients are usually co-treated for Chlamydia as well, which includes adding azithromycin or doxycycline to the ceftriaxone.
bottom_line: This patient has disseminated gonococcal infection (DGI), a complication of Neisseria gonorrhoeae bacteremia.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 76-year-old man is evaluated for worsening diarrhea during his hospital stay. He was admitted 9 days ago for an acute ischemic stroke and has been receiving supportive care, including aspirin, atorvastatin, and physical therapy. Due to concern for aspiration pneumonia early in his admission, he was empirically started on intravenous ceftriaxone and oral azithromycin, which were discontinued two days ago. Over the past 48 hours, nursing staff report that the patient has been having frequent loose, watery stools — approximately 5 to 6 episodes per day — which are nonbloody and foul-smelling. He has developed mild diffuse abdominal discomfort, reduced appetite, and occasional dizziness when ambulating. He is afebrile, hemodynamically stable, and fully oriented, though he appears tired. On physical exam, his mucous membranes are dry, and the abdomen is soft and nondistended with mild generalized tenderness but no rebound or guarding. Bowel sounds are present and hyperactive. Labs reveal a leukocyte count of 14,600/mm³. Stool studies are obtained. He is placed on contact isolation, and IV fluids are started. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
This patient presents with new-onset watery diarrhea developing during a prolonged hospitalization following recent antibiotic exposure— ceftriaxone and azithromycin. The timing (9 days after starting antibiotics) along with leukocytosis raises high suspicion for Clostridium difficile infection (CDI).
CDI occurs when antibiotics disrupt the normal colonic flora, creating an opportunity for ingested C. difficile spores to colonize the bowel. The bacteria produce toxins A and B, which cause cytoskeletal disruption in enterocytes, leading to mucosal inflammation, increased intestinal secretion, and the characteristic watery or bloody diarrhea. Elderly, hospitalized patients—especially those with comorbidities or immunosuppression
—are at highest risk.
The most appropriate first-line treatment for a patient with CDI who is clinically stable and able to tolerate oral medications is oral vancomycin. Despite its generally poor oral bioavailability, this pharmacokinetic profile is actually advantageous in CDI because vancomycin remains in the gastrointestinal lumen, concentrating at the site of infection. It directly inhibits C. difficile cell wall synthesis, reduces toxin production, and
promotes bacterial clearance without systemic absorption.
Compared to older regimens such as metronidazole, oral vancomycin has demonstrated superior efficacy and lower recurrence rates, particularly in moderate-to-severe infections and in older adults.
Stool toxin testing, which identifies toxins A and B, is the gold-standard diagnostic test.
Supportive measures are also essential in the management of CDI. These include fluid resuscitation, correction of electrolyte imbalances, cessation of inciting antibiotics if clinically possible, and implementation of strict infection control measures (eg, contact precautions, handwashing with soap to remove spores).
bottom_line: Suspect C. difficile infection in hospitalized or elderly patients with recent antibiotic exposure and new-onset diarrhea. Diagnose with stool AB toxin test. Oral vancomycin is the first-line treatment due to its localized action in the GI tract. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
topic: Cimetidine causes gynecomastia. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System
stem: A 56-year-old man comes to the physician because of decreased libido and bilateral breast tenderness that has worsened over the past several weeks. He has a history of peptic ulcer disease and hypertension. He takes hydrochlorothiazide and another over-the-counter medication for his ulcer symptoms, which he started 2 months ago. Physical examination shows bilateral gynecomastia without galactorrhea or testicular abnormalities. Serum testosterone is slightly increased, and prolactin is normal. Which of the following medications is most likely responsible for this patient’s symptoms?
explanation:
Cimetidine, an H2 receptor antagonist used to decrease stomach acid secretion, can cause gynecomastia due to anti-androgenic effects at breast tissue. Impotence, menstrual irregularities, and decreased libido can also be reported in some patients as well.
Blockade of androgen receptors can result in compensatory increased serum levels. This is not seen in all patients, however, as androgens can be normal or even decreased depending on the patient.
Cimetidine is also a strong inhibitor of P-450, meaning patients taking other drugs, such as warfarin and TCAs, might need to have their doses adjusted. For example, the USMLE might tell you a patient taking warfarin started taking a second drug, and then their INR goes up (meaning the warfarin is "too strong" now), and they might asking what the second drug added was → answer = cimetidine (as an example).
bottom_line: Cimetidine, an H2 receptor antagonist used to decrease stomach acid secretion, can cause gynecomastia due to anti-androgenic effects at breast tissue.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacologic therapy to initiate at this time?
explanation:
Wilson disease is an autosomal recessive defect of ATP7B, a copper-transporting ATPase in hepatocytes, leading to impaired incorporation of copper into ceruloplasmin and defective biliary copper excretion. This results in progressive copper accumulation in the liver, brain (especially basal ganglia), cornea (Kayser–Fleischer rings), and other tissues. In symptomatic patients—especially those with both hepatic and neurologic
manifestations—penicillamine is first-line for induction therapy. It is a thiol-containing chelating agent that binds copper, forming soluble complexes excreted in urine, thereby reducing tissue burden. Early initiation of chelation can halt disease progression and, in many cases, improve both hepatic and neurologic outcomes.
Penicillamine therapy typically begins at a low dose to minimize neurologic worsening from rapid copper mobilization, then titrated up to achieve effective urinary copper excretion. Because penicillamine can cause adverse effects such as rash, proteinuria, leukopenia, and drug-induced lupus, careful monitoring is required, and pyridoxine supplementation is recommended to prevent deficiency. If penicillamine is not tolerated,
trientine is a suitable alternative chelator. Chelation is typically continued until copper levels are well controlled, followed by transition to maintenance therapy—often zinc salts—to prevent re-accumulation.
Long-term management of Wilson disease pairs pharmacologic therapy with lifestyle modifications to reduce copper intake, including avoidance of shellfish, organ meats, chocolate, nuts, and certain well water. Family members should be screened with genetic testing and/or biochemical studies, as early treatment in asymptomatic carriers can prevent irreversible damage. In advanced cases with fulminant hepatic failure or
decompensated cirrhosis unresponsive to chelation, liver transplantation is curative by replacing the defective ATP7B enzyme. However, in this symptomatic adolescent, oral penicillamine is the correct initial choice to chelate copper and reduce the toxic burden.
bottom_line: Wilson disease causes copper accumulation due to defective biliary excretion, leading to hepatic, neurologic, and psychiatric symptoms. Symptomatic patients require immediate copper chelation—penicillamine is first-line—to rapidly increase urinary excretion and reduce toxic tissue burden, followed by maintenance therapy with zinc to prevent recurrence. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic therapy?
explanation:
Chronic arsenic poisoning classically presents with dermatologic changes (hyperpigmentation and hypopigmentation in a “raindrop” pattern), peripheral neuropathy (stocking–glove sensory loss and burning pain), and Mees lines—transverse white bands on the fingernails that represent disrupted nail matrix keratinization. Arsenic exerts toxicity by binding to sulfhydryl groups, inhibiting multiple enzymes in cellular respiration,
including pyruvate dehydrogenase, which impairs oxidative phosphorylation and ATP production. Chronic exposure is often occupational (pesticides, insecticides, contaminated water) and can increase the risk of skin and lung cancers. Dimercaprol is a chelating agent that binds arsenic via sulfhydryl groups, forming a stable complex excreted in urine. It is the first-line treatment for acute and significant chronic arsenic toxicity,
particularly when neurologic and dermatologic manifestations are present.
Chelation with dimercaprol interrupts arsenic’s inhibition of lipoic acid–dependent enzymes, thereby restoring mitochondrial oxidative metabolism and halting progression of neurologic and systemic symptoms. Early initiation is crucial, as prolonged exposure can lead to irreversible neuropathy and malignancy. Other chelators like succimer may be used in less severe cases or in children, but dimercaprol remains standard for
more advanced presentations with systemic involvement. In addition to chelation, removal from the exposure source, occupational safety measures, and public health interventions are essential to prevent recurrence. This patient’s work with pesticides and the combination of neuropathy, skin changes, and Mees lines make arsenic poisoning the most likely diagnosis.
bottom_line: Chronic arsenic poisoning presents with skin pigmentation changes, symmetric peripheral neuropathy, and Mees lines on the nails. Dimercaprol is the first-line chelator for significant arsenic toxicity, particularly when systemic manifestations are present. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the outpatient clinic with ongoing trouble managing her daily stress and persistent anxiety. She explains that for the past 10–12 months, she has felt "constantly on edge," even when nothing in particular is wrong. She says her thoughts often race about worst-case scenarios, including minor things like whether she forgot to unplug her curling iron or whether her car tires might burst during a short drive. She reports difficulty falling asleep most nights and sometimes wakes up in the middle of the night, her mind filled with unfinished to-do lists or imagined emergencies. She describes fatigue during the day, difficulty concentrating at work, and increasingly frequent tension headaches. Although her job as a graphic designer allows flexibility, she finds herself mentally exhausted by midday and has begun avoiding team meetings due to a fear of being judged if she forgets to contribute something important. She recently spent three weeks traveling out of state, hoping a change of scenery would provide some relief. She visited friends in Colorado, drove through Utah to visit national parks, and finished the trip in Arizona for a desert pottery retreat. Though she enjoyed these activities at times, she admits the anxiety persisted during the trip—she even spent hours comparing emergency vet clinics online when one of her friend’s dogs had diarrhea. She’s developed a growing interest in collecting rare hot sauces from each region she visits and has over 200 bottles displayed in her kitchen. Twice a week, she attends a local pottery studio, which she finds therapeutic when she can focus. She also recently began investing in contemporary art through an online platform, though she says the decision has created more stress than she anticipated. On review of systems, she denies panic attacks, compulsions, hallucinations, or flashbacks. Her appetite is normal, though she’s noticed some jaw tension and mild nausea during especially anxious days. She drinks socially but does not use recreational drugs and takes no medications. She is in a stable relationship and has no psychiatric history. On mental status exam, she appears mildly restless but cooperative, with appropriate affect and logical thought processes. She agrees to begin treatment and expresses a desire to avoid medication that causes sedation or dependence. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
explanation:
Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) such as sertraline are first-line pharmacologic treatment for generalized anxiety disorder (GAD). They help reduce the core symptoms of worry, restlessness, irritability, and somatic tension by increasing synaptic serotonin levels. Sertraline has a well-established safety profile, is non-sedating, and lacks the abuse potential associated with benzodiazepines, making it appropriate for
patients seeking long-term therapy. SSRIs typically take several weeks to become effective, so patients should be counseled to expect a gradual improvement and not abrupt relief. Side effects may include transient gastrointestinal upset, insomnia, or sexual dysfunction, but these often diminish with time.
GAD is a chronic condition that requires both pharmacologic and psychotherapeutic intervention. SSRIs like sertraline are used for moderate to severe symptoms, and their use is evidence-based and FDA-approved for anxiety disorders. They are safe for long-term use and can be titrated slowly to minimize adverse effects. In cases where patients do not respond to one SSRI, a trial of another SSRI or SNRI (e.g., venlafaxine or
duloxetine) is appropriate. Importantly, the goal is to reduce functional impairment while avoiding medications that carry a risk of dependence or adverse metabolic profiles.
Though many patients benefit from combination therapy with cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT), SSRIs remain the most effective monotherapy for pharmacologic treatment. The decision to begin medication may depend on symptom severity, patient preference, and access to psychotherapy. In this case, sertraline is an appropriate first-line option due to the chronicity and impact of symptoms, her openness to treatment, and
her concern about dependence.
bottom_line: Generalized anxiety disorder should be treated initially with SSRIs or cognitive behavioral therapy, depending on severity and patient preference. Sertraline is an effective, well-tolerated, and safe first-line pharmacologic agent for patients with chronic excessive worry. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 25-year-old previously healthy woman comes to the clinic with 4 days of cough, low-grade fever, malaise, and mild shortness of breath. She says the cough is dry and associated with mild chest discomfort but no hemoptysis. She recently returned from a weekend hiking trip with friends and denies sick contacts. She has no history of asthma or other lung disease, and she takes no medications. On examination, her temperature is 38.1°C (100.6°F), pulse is 92/min, blood pressure is 112/68 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation is 96% on room air. Lungs reveal scattered crackles and faint expiratory wheezes in the left upper lung field. There is no egophony or dullness to percussion. Chest x-ray shows a left upper lobe patchy infiltrate without pleural effusion. Which of the following is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic choice for this patient?
explanation:
Azithromycin is a macrolide antibiotic that is often used as empiric outpatient treatment for community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in otherwise healthy adults without comorbidities. On USMLE, the key reason is that macrolides provide coverage for both Strep pneumoniae (the most common cause of CAP) and atypical pathogens (Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Chlamydophila pneumoniae, Legionella pneumophila). Beta-lactams
like amoxicillin and penicillin cover typical bacteria but not atypicals because atypicals lack a peptidoglycan cell wall, the target of beta-lactam antibiotics.
Macrolides inhibit bacterial protein synthesis by binding to the 50S ribosomal subunit and blocking translocation of the ribosome along mRNA. This is a bacteriostatic mechanism that halts protein production, ultimately preventing bacterial growth. Azithromycin is particularly useful because it has excellent intracellular penetration (important for atypicals, which are often intracellular) and a long half-life, allowing once-daily dosing.
For young, otherwise healthy patients with mild CAP, a macrolide alone is appropriate unless local resistance rates are high.
In addition to respiratory coverage, azithromycin is well tolerated, with possible adverse effects including QT prolongation, gastrointestinal upset, and rare cholestatic hepatitis. Resistance develops through methylation of the 23S rRNA binding site or active efflux pumps in bacteria. In outpatient CAP, macrolide monotherapy remains a HY first-line option for USMLE questions when both typical and atypical pathogens need to be
covered in otherwise healthy patients.
bottom_line: For otherwise healthy adults with mild outpatient community-acquired pneumonia, empiric therapy should cover both S. pneumo and atypicals. Macrolides such as azithromycin achieve this with a 50S ribosomal subunit mechanism, while beta-lactams fail to cover atypicals due to their lack of peptidoglycan. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
topic: Clozapine causes agranulocytosis. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System
stem: A 41-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by a community outreach volunteer because of fever, chills, and sore throat. He says that the throat pain started 3 days ago and has been getting progressively worse. He also reports fatigue, malaise, and painful swallowing that prevents him from eating much solid food. He denies cough, chest pain, or shortness of breath. His past psychiatric history is extensive, including diagnoses of schizophrenia, depression, and generalized anxiety disorder. He has been hospitalized multiple times in psychiatric wards since his late teens, often due to auditory hallucinations and disorganized behavior. Over the years, he has been tried on multiple medications including haloperidol, risperidone, quetiapine, fluoxetine, and lithium. Due to persistent refractory psychosis, his care team eventually initiated clozapine therapy, which he has been taking for the past year. He is also prescribed valproic acid as a mood stabilizer, lorazepam as needed for agitation, and sertraline for comorbid depressive symptoms. He lost touch with his family years ago after repeated conflicts and strained relationships, and he currently has no fixed residence. He usually sleeps outside near a convenience store in Brickell, Miami. He describes this as “the safest spot I could find” and says that store clerks occasionally give him food. On days when he does not stay in Brickell, he sometimes travels to Wynwood and sleeps near an abandoned building, where he feels somewhat shielded from the rain. He frequently wanders shopping streets in both neighborhoods, asking strangers for spare change. He admits that his lifestyle makes it difficult to keep up with medical appointments, and he often skips his medications depending on availability. Despite these challenges, he has continued clozapine almost daily because, in his words, “it’s the only one that really makes the voices quiet.” Over the past week, however, he has felt progressively more fatigued. He developed subjective fevers, chills, and oral discomfort. Two days ago he noticed painful ulcerations on his gums and tongue. Today, he feels too weak to walk long distances and accepted the help of a volunteer who insisted he be brought to the hospital. On review of systems, he denies chest pain, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, diarrhea, or skin rashes. He does not smoke cigarettes but occasionally drinks alcohol when he has access to it. He does not use illicit drugs currently but has a remote history of marijuana and cocaine use. On physical examination, he appears tired and somewhat disheveled.
