Febuxostat is a non-purine selective inhibitor, often used in patients who cannot tolerate allopurinol. It helps prevent future gout flares by lowering serum urate levels. This makes it useful for long-term gout management rather than
acute attacks. Some recent studies have shown febuxostat to be more effective than allopurinol.
Gout is an inflammatory arthritis caused by monosodium urate crystal deposition, most often due to hyperuricemia. The classic initial presentation is podagra, or acute monoarthritis of the first metatarsophalangeal joint (big toe), which becomes red, swollen, and extremely tender. On polarized light microscopy, the crystals appear as needle-shaped and negatively birefringent, meaning they appear yellow when aligned parallel to
the light. Gout most commonly affects middle-aged to older men, though it can also occur in postmenopausal women or those with renal disease or diuretic use.
Xanthine oxidase inhibitors like allopurinol and febuxostat are not used to treat acute gout flares because they can transiently alter serum urate levels and destabilize existing urate deposits, potentially worsening or prolonging the attack. So these agents are only used for chronic gout to decrease recurrences. Students mess this up so I'll reiterate it.
Other random factoids USMLE likes for xanthine oxidase inhibitors: 1) They're used to prevent tumor lysis syndrome (i.e., renal failure when treating leukemia), by decreasing the rapid uric acid production seen with lysis of leukemic cells; and 2) Don't combine 6-mercaptopurine with xanthine oxidase inhibitors, since 6-MP requires XO for breakdown. In other words, if we want to prevent tumor lysis syndrome by giving a XOI, we
don't do this if patient is on 6-MP since they can get 6-MP toxicity.
bottom_line: Febuxostat and allopurinol are xanthine oxidase inhibitors used for chronic gout to decrease recurrence of flares. They can also be used to prevent tumor lysis syndrome. Do not give if a patient is taking 6-mercaptopurine. Pharmacology Subject Biochemistry (General Principles) (DNA, RNA, nucleic acid) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 56-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department by her husband after developing sudden weakness of the right side of her body and difficulty speaking earlier today. She has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Her temperature is 36.8°C, blood pressure is 150/84 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respiratory rate is 16/min. Neurologic examination reveals expressive aphasia, right facial droop sparing the forehead, and decreased strength in the right upper and lower extremities. Non-contrast CT of the head shows no evidence of hemorrhage. The onset of her symptoms was approximately 6 hours ago. She is started on medical therapy to reduce further neurologic injury. Use of this medication is contraindicated in which of the following conditions?
explanation:
This patient is receiving aspirin for ischemic stroke, as it is indicated when presentation occurs more than 4.5 hours after symptom onset. Aspirin is contraindicated in children with viral illnesses due to the risk of Reye syndrome. Reye syndrome is a life-threatening condition characterized by acute hepatic steatosis and cerebral edema resulting from defective beta-oxidation, and it can occur when children are given aspirin during
or after viral infections such as influenza or varicella. Therefore, aspirin is avoided in pediatric patients to prevent this potentially fatal complication.
Irreversible cyclooxygenase (COX) 1&2 inhibitor is the mechanism of aspirin, which is classified pharmacologically as a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID). It is also known as salicylic acid (salicylate). Aspirin blocks the synthesis of prostaglandins and platelet thromboxane A₂. Decreased prostaglandin leads to reduced inflammation, pain, and body temperature, while decreased thromboxane A₂ reduces platelet
clumping, lowering thromboembolic risk.
Aspirin has multiple HY clinical indications on USMLE. 1) In atrial fibrillation, patients with a CHADS₂ score of 0 or 1 are given aspirin to prevent stroke, while warfarin is used for scores of 2 or higher. 2) In peripheral vascular disease and carotid stenosis, aspirin is part of the standard management triad alongside a statin and an ACE inhibitor or ARB to reduce cardiovascular events. 3) In ischemic heart disease, aspirin is used
daily for secondary prevention in patients with stable angina or prior myocardial infarction. 4) In ischemic stroke management, aspirin is administered if more than 4.5 hours have passed since symptom onset, whereas tPA is used if within 4.5 hours.
Aspirin also has important adverse effects and contraindications. It can cause gastrointestinal bleeding due to decreased gastric mucosal protection (decreased prostaglandin), nephropathy manifesting as pre-renal AKI, interstitial nephritis, or nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, and asthma by COX inhibition shunting arachidonic acid metabolism towards leukotrienes. It is contraindicated in children because of Reye syndrome, a
defect in beta-oxidation leading to cerebral edema and hepatitis. Misoprostol, a PGE₁ analogue, is used alongside or after PPIs in patients with NSAID-induced gastric ulcers. High doses of aspirin can cause a high-anion gap mixed metabolic acidosis and respiratory alkalosis, represented by the S in MUDPILES.
bottom_line: Aspirin is indicated in ischemic stroke presenting after 4.5 hours to prevent further thrombus propagation. It is contraindicated in children with viral illnesses due to the risk of Reye syndrome, which causes hepatic steatosis and cerebral edema. Pharmacology Subject Biochemistry (General Principles) (Lipid metabolism) System None Topic Block Time Remaining: 01:15:00 TIMED

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his roommate due to spontaneous gum bleeding and extensive bruising on his arms and legs. He has no history of bleeding disorders and takes no medications. The roommate reports that the patient has been increasingly reclusive over the past few weeks and may have ingested something harmful, although he denies any specific knowledge. The patient admits to feeling depressed but denies taking any prescription drugs or supplements. He does not consume alcohol and has no liver disease. His vitals are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals several ecchymoses on the upper extremities, oozing from the gums, and no petechiae. There is no hepatosplenomegaly. A serum toxicology screen is negative for opioids, benzodiazepines, and acetaminophen. Further questioning reveals that the patient had been attempting to poison rats in the apartment using over-the-counter rodenticides several days prior. Laboratory studies are shown: Platelets                                                          180,000/μL Bleeding time                                                   5 minutes Prothrombin time (PT)                                     24 seconds Partial thromboplastin time (PTT)                    61 seconds Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?

options.C: Impaired activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X
options.D: Platelet adhesion defect due to glycoprotein Ib deficiency

answer_text: Impaired activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X
explanation:
This patient is experiencing a bleeding disorder caused by vitamin K deficiency, most likely due to ingestion of rodenticide. Many commercial rodenticides contain superwarfarins (e.g., brodifacoum), which are long-acting vitamin K antagonists. Like warfarin, these compounds inhibit vitamin K epoxide reductase, an enzyme that recycles oxidized vitamin K into its reduced, active form in the liver.
Reduced vitamin K is essential for the gamma-carboxylation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X. Without this modification, the clotting factors cannot bind calcium and participate in the coagulation cascade, leading to impaired thrombin generation and poor fibrin clot formation.
The clinical consequence is a prolonged PT and aPTT, with normal platelet count and bleeding time—a hallmark pattern of a coagulation factor deficiency, not platelet dysfunction. The PT typically rises first due to the short half-life of factor VII but with continued exposure or depletion, both PT and aPTT are affected. Since platelets are unaffected, bleeding time remains normal.
Bleeding symptoms may range from mucosal oozing and ecchymoses to life-threatening hemorrhages. Ingestion of superwarfarin results in delayed but persistent coagulopathy, and treatment requires high-dose and often prolonged vitamin K therapy, sometimes for weeks, given the long half-life of these compounds.
Recognition of this mechanism is critical in the setting of unexplained coagulopathy, especially when toxic ingestion is suspected. Superwarfarin poisoning should be considered when standard coagulation studies are abnormal but platelet count and liver function are normal, and the patient has potential access to rodenticides. Confirmatory diagnosis may require specialized toxicology testing, but empiric treatment with vitamin K
should not be delayed when the clinical picture is suggestive.
bottom_line: Ingestion of rodenticides containing superwarfarins causes vitamin K deficiency by inhibiting vitamin K epoxide reductase, impairing activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X. This leads to prolonged PT and aPTT with normal platelets and bleeding time. Pharmacology Subject Biochemistry (General Principles) (Vitamins, cofactors, nutrients) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Which of the following is the next best step in management?
explanation:
This patient presents with a painful vesicular lesion on the finger accompanied by low-grade fever and regional lymphadenopathy, strongly suggestive of herpetic whitlow. Herpetic whitlow is caused by direct inoculation of herpes simplex virus (HSV) into the skin, typically through minor trauma.
It is most often seen in healthcare workers such as dentists due to frequent exposure to oral secretions. Diagnosis is clinical and based on characteristic grouped vesicles, pain, and swelling localized to a single digit. Although herpetic whitlow is usually self-limited, treatment with oral acyclovir is indicated in cases presenting within 48 hours or in immunocompromised patients to reduce duration and severity.
Acyclovir is a guanosine analog that selectively inhibits viral DNA polymerase. It must first be activated by viral thymidine kinase, followed by host cell kinases, into its active triphosphate form. Once activated, acyclovir competes with deoxyguanosine triphosphate during viral DNA replication, ultimately causing DNA chain termination. Its selectivity for virally infected cells limits toxicity to host cells. Early initiation of oral acyclovir
in herpetic whitlow leads to faster resolution of symptoms and reduced viral shedding, particularly in healthcare workers where nosocomial spread is a concern.
bottom_line: Herpetic whitlow is a viral infection of the distal fingers caused by HSV, typically seen in healthcare workers. It presents with pain, erythema, and grouped vesicles. Treatment includes oral acyclovir/valacyclovir. Pharmacology Subject Dermatology (Infection-related presentations) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, profound fatigue, and painful sores in his mouth. He was treated with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for a skin abscess 10 days ago. On examination, he has a temperature of 38.9°C (102°F) and multiple ulcerative lesions along the gingival margin and buccal mucosa. Blood cultures and empiric antibiotics are initiated. Which of the following findings is most directly related to this patient's mouth sores?
explanation:
This patient has neutropenic fever (aka febrile neutropenia), which is the combo of low neutrophils + fever.
Because neutrophils are essential for containing bacterial and fungal pathogens, even minor infections can rapidly progress to sepsis. It is considered a medical emergency requiring immediate empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is a sulfonamide antibiotic that, in rare cases, causes idiosyncratic agranulocytosis due to direct bone marrow suppression or immune-mediated destruction of granulocyte precursors.
Profound neutropenia compromises innate immunity, especially at mucosal surfaces constantly exposed to bacterial colonization. As neutrophil counts drop below 500/mm³, patients often develop painful oral ulcerations and gingival inflammation, which are early signs of infection risk.
Mouth ulcers (aka mucositis, or ulcerative pharyngitis) is the highest yield way neutropenia shows up on USMLE. They can also just say the patient has sore throat + a fever.
Without prompt intervention, these lesions can progress to invasive bacterial or fungal sepsis. Other medications—like methimazole, propylthiouracil, clozapine, and methotrexate—are also known triggers of agranulocytosis.
Unlike simple antibiotic rash or hypersensitivity reactions, this life-threatening toxicity requires immediate broad-spectrum antibiotics and hematology support. Recognizing mouth ulcers as a warning sign is critical for early diagnosis.
bottom_line: The highest yield presentation for neutropenia on USMLE is mouth ulcers. TMP/SMX, clozapine, methimazole, propylthiouracil, and methotrexate are the most common drugs causing agranulocytosis for USMLE. Pharmacology Subject Hematology & Oncology (Toxic drug effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 35-year-old man presents with redness, swelling, and pain in his left hand that began 2 days ago after he cut himself while using tools. On physical exam, the dorsum of the left hand is erythematous, warm, and tender. His temperature is 38.6°C (101.5°F), heart rate is 72/min, blood pressure is 120/76 mmHg, and respiratory rate is 14/min. Treatment is initiated with oral dicloxacillin, but his symptoms fail to improve. Wound cultures later grow MRSA. Production of which of the following is most likely responsible for this organism’s resistance?
explanation:
Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) acquires resistance to β-lactam antibiotics through horizontal gene transfer of the mecA gene, which encodes PBP2a – an altered penicillin-binding protein (PBP). Unlike native PBPs, PBP2a has a structurally modified active site with low affinity for β-lactams (e.g., methicillin, oxacillin, nafcillin), allowing continued transpeptidase activity and peptidoglycan cross-linking despite
the presence of antibiotics. This mechanism represents target site modification, not antibiotic inactivation (e.g., β-lactamase), and renders the entire β-lactam class ineffective.
The reason we know β-lactamase is wrong is because methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA) already produces beta-lactamase. Therefore, the transformation of MSSA into MRSA cannot be due to increased beta-lactamase production, since that resistance mechanism is already present in MSSA.
Vancomycin is ordinarily used for MRSA, e.g., in the setting of endocarditis. For skin infections caused by MRSA, agents other than vancomycin are often used, such as clindamycin, because vancomycin has poor skin penetration.
bottom_line: MRSA is resistant to all β-lactam antibiotics due to the acquisition of the mecA gene, which encodes an altered penicillin-binding protein (PBP2a) with low affinity for β-lactams. This structural target modification prevents drug binding and allows continued cell wall synthesis, necessitating treatment with non–β-lactam agents like vancomycin. Pharmacology Subject Microbiology (General Principles) (Bacteriology) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 34-year-old woman presents to the clinic complaining of a persistent vaginal odor and thin grayish discharge for the past 5 days. She denies itching, burning, or dyspareunia. Her last menstrual period was 2 weeks ago, and she is not currently using any contraception. She reports having a new sexual partner within the last month and uses douches regularly. On pelvic examination, the vaginal mucosa appears normal, but there is a thin homogenous discharge coating the vaginal walls. A positive whiff test is noted when 10% potassium hydroxide is applied to the discharge. Microscopy of the vaginal fluid reveals clue cells. The patient is afebrile and has no abdominal tenderness or cervical motion tenderness. Pregnancy test is negative. She is not allergic to any medications. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Reassure and provide hygiene counseling only
options.E: Treat empirically with intramuscular ceftriaxone and oral doxycycline

explanation:
Oral metronidazole is the treatment of choice for bacterial vaginosis (BV), a condition caused by an imbalance in the normal vaginal flora, most commonly associated with Gardnerella vaginalis.
BV presents with a characteristic thin, gray, malodorous discharge and is diagnosed using Amsel criteria: presence of clue cells, vaginal pH >4.5, positive amine (“whiff”) test, and characteristic discharge. Treatment is recommended even in asymptomatic patients if they are pregnant or undergoing gynecologic procedures. Although metronidazole can be given vaginally or orally, oral treatment is often preferred in nonpregnant
women due to ease of administration and similar efficacy. It is important to advise patients to abstain from alcohol while taking metronidazole due to a potential disulfiram-like reaction.
Additionally, sexual partners do not require treatment for BV, as it is not considered a sexually transmitted infection, although multiple or new partners increase risk. Patient education about avoiding douching and maintaining healthy vaginal flora is important in preventing recurrence. Recurrence rates are high, and follow-up is only necessary if symptoms persist. Intravaginal treatments are effective but may be less appropriate
in settings requiring systemic therapy.
Clinical awareness is critical, especially when differentiating from conditions like candidiasis or trichomoniasis.
bottom_line: Bacterial vaginosis presents with thin gray discharge, clue cells on microscopy, elevated vaginal pH, and positive whiff test. Oral metronidazole is the treatment of choice, and unnecessary antibiotics or antifungals should be avoided. Pharmacology Subject Microbiology (General Principles) (Bacteriology) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 70-year-old woman comes to the ophthalmology clinic due to progressive vision changes over the past 10 days. She reports that her vision has become “washed out” and that colors appear faded, especially reds and greens. She also has increasing difficulty seeing details directly in front of her. Two months ago, she was diagnosed with active pulmonary tuberculosis, confirmed by sputum culture, and started on standard multi-drug therapy. Her only other medication is lisinopril for hypertension. She does not smoke or drink alcohol. Visual acuity is 20/60 bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam is normal. Visual field testing reveals central scotomas and red-green color discrimination impairment. Peripheral vision is intact. Which of the following medications is the most likely cause of her symptoms?
explanation:
Ethambutol is a first-line antimycobacterial agent used in RIPE therapy (rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol) for tuberculosis. It inhibits arabinosyl transferase, disrupting carbohydrate polymerization in the mycobacterial cell wall.
A classic adverse effect is optic neuropathy, which presents with painless bilateral central vision loss, central scotomas, and red-green dyschromatopsia (a fancy term for color blindness). Peripheral vision is often preserved, and the funduscopic exam may appear normal, especially in early stages.
These visual symptoms typically develop after weeks to months of therapy, particularly in elderly patients or those with renal impairment. Ethambutol toxicity is dose-dependent and can result in permanent vision loss if not promptly recognized and the drug discontinued. Importantly, none of the other RIPE agents cause optic neuritis, which helps narrow the differential diagnosis.
While all RIPE drugs can cause hepatotoxicity, only ethambutol affects the optic nerve. The USMLE frequently tests the association between optic symptoms during RIPE therapy and ethambutol, emphasizing that the drug should be discontinued immediately if toxicity is suspected.
bottom_line: Ethambutol causes optic neuropathy characterized by painless bilateral central vision loss, red-green color blindness (dyschromatopsia), and central scotomas with preserved peripheral vision and a normal fundus exam—making it the only RIPE agent associated with optic toxicity and one that must be discontinued immediately if symptoms arise. Pharmacology Subject Microbiology (General Principles) (Bacteriology) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Which of the following fungal cellular locations is most consistent with the molecular target of this pharmacologic therapy?
explanation:
This patient has invasive aspergillosis, a life-threatening opportunistic fungal infection that commonly develops in severely immunocompromised patients, particularly those with prolonged neutropenia after cytotoxic chemotherapy. Echinocandins such as caspofungin and micafungin inhibit β-1,3-D-glucan synthase, a key enzyme required for the production of β-glucan, an essential carbohydrate component of the fungal cell wall.
Without β-glucan, the cell wall loses its structural integrity, making the fungus vulnerable to osmotic stress and ultimately leading to cell lysis. Because human cells lack cell walls and β-glucan, echinocandins selectively target fungi without directly harming human cells, giving them a favorable safety profile compared with some other systemic antifungals.
Caspofungin is administered intravenously due to poor oral bioavailability and is generally well tolerated; potential adverse effects include histamine-mediated flushing, gastrointestinal upset, and elevated liver enzymes. Echinocandins are highly effective against Candida species and have fungistatic activity against Aspergillus. In invasive aspergillosis, voriconazole is often first-line, but echinocandins are valuable as salvage
therapy or when azole use is limited by drug resistance, interactions, or intolerance. The key clue in this case is the description of a drug that inhibits β-1,3-D-glucan carbohydrate synthesis in the cell wall — a mechanism unique to echinocandins like caspofungin and micafungin.
bottom_line: Caspofungin and micafungin are echinocandins that inhibit β-1,3-D-glucan carbohydrate fungal cell wall synthesis. Pharmacology Subject Microbiology (General Principles) (Mycology) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Which of the following fungal cellular locations is most consistent with the molecular target of this pharmacologic therapy?
explanation:
This patient has oral candidiasis (thrush), most often caused by Candida albicans. Risk factors include recent antibiotic use, immunosuppression, diabetes, and inhaled corticosteroid use without proper mouth rinsing afterward, as retained steroid residue in the oropharynx promotes local immunosuppression and fungal overgrowth. Nystatin is not absorbed systemically when used orally, making it ideal for localized infections of
the oral cavity and gastrointestinal tract. In oral thrush, nystatin is typically given as a suspension that is swished around the mouth to maximize contact with lesions before swallowing, also covering potential esophageal involvement. Because of its local action and minimal systemic absorption, nystatin has very few systemic side effects and is considered first-line for mild oral candidiasis. Patients who use inhaled corticosteroids
for asthma should be counseled to rinse their mouths after each use to prevent this common complication.
Nystatin’s mechanism of action involves binding directly to ergosterol, a critical sterol component of fungal cell membranes. By binding to ergosterol, nystatin forms transmembrane pores, causing leakage of intracellular ions and metabolites, which rapidly leads to fungal cell death. This fungicidal activity is highly selective for fungi because mammalian cell membranes contain cholesterol instead of ergosterol, minimizing toxicity.
However, because nystatin is too toxic for systemic use, it is reserved for topical or oral (non-absorbed) applications such as oral thrush, cutaneous candidiasis, or GI fungal infections. This targeted mechanism allows for effective eradication of Candida in the oral cavity while avoiding systemic exposure.
Amphotericin B has a mechanism of action similar to nystatin in that it binds to ergosterol in fungal cell membranes, creating pores that disrupt membrane integrity and cause leakage of intracellular components, ultimately leading to cell death. However, unlike nystatin, amphotericin B can be administered systemically, allowing it to treat severe, life-threatening fungal infections such as cryptococcal meningitis, disseminated
histoplasmosis, mucormycosis, and invasive Aspergillus or Candida infections. It can be given intravenously for systemic disease or intrathecally for fungal CNS infections. Despite its broad antifungal spectrum, its use is limited by significant toxicities, particularly nephrotoxicity, electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia), and infusion-related reactions (fever, chills, hypotension). Because of these risks,
amphotericin B is often reserved for serious infections where less toxic antifungals are ineffective or contraindicated.
bottom_line: This patient has oral candidiasis (thrush), most often caused by Candida albicans.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with nausea, vomiting, and right upper quadrant pain 16 hours after an intentional overdose of acetaminophen. She reports taking “a whole bottle” but cannot recall how many pills. Her temperature is 99 F, blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, and pulse is 90/min. On examination, she is alert but uncomfortable, with tenderness to palpation over the right upper quadrant. Laboratory studies show AST of 3,200 U/L and ALT of 3,600 U/L; total bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase are normal. INR is mildly elevated at 1.7. The patient is admitted and started on N-acetylcysteine (NAC) therapy. Which of the following best justifies the use of NAC in this patient's acute management?

options.A: Enhances cytochrome P450-mediated metabolism of acetaminophen
options.C: Promotes renal excretion of unmetabolized acetaminophen

bottom_line: A 22-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with nausea, vomiting, and right upper quadrant pain 16 hours after an intentional overdose of acetaminophen. She reports taking “a whole bottle” but cannot recall how many pills. Her temperature is 99 F, blood pressure is 110/70 mm Hg, and pulse is 90/min. On examination, she is alert but uncomfortable, with tenderness to palpation over the right upper quadrant. Laboratory studies show AST of 3,200 U/L and ALT of 3,600 U/L; total bilirubin and alkaline phosphatase are normal. INR is mildly elevated at 1.7. The patient is admitted and started on N-acetylcysteine (NAC) therapy. Which of the following best justifies the use of NAC in this patient's acute management? A. Enhances cytochrome P450-mediated metabolism of acetaminophen B. Inhibits enterohepatic circulation of NAPQI C. Promotes renal excretion of unmetabolized acetaminophen D. Regenerates intracellular reduced glutathione E. Stimulates glucuronidation of acetaminophen

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 72-year-old man presents for follow-up due to worsening back pain and weight loss. He has a history of urinary hesitancy and nocturia. Bone scan reveals multiple areas of increased uptake in the lumbar spine and pelvis. Prostate exam reveals a firm, nodular prostate. Serum prostate-specific antigen is markedly elevated. A biopsy confirms adenocarcinoma. He is started on pharmacologic therapy that initially increases serum testosterone levels before resulting in desensitization. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of the drug used to treat this patient’s condition?
explanation:
Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonists (i.e., leuprolide or goserelin) are first-line agents for metastatic prostate cancer. These drugs initially stimulate pituitary GnRH receptors, leading to a transient increase in LH and testosterone, but with continuous use, they cause receptor downregulation and desensitization, reducing LH and FSH release and effectively shutting down the hypothalamic-pituitary axis. The result is
suppression of testicular androgen production, which is critical because prostate cancer is androgen-dependent.
This mechanism contrasts with androgen receptor blockers (i.e., flutamide), which are often given concurrently to nullify the effects of the transiently increased testosterone levels.
bottom_line: Leuprolide and goserelin are examples of GnRH receptor agonists that initially cause a transient increase in LH and androgen levels before desensitization at the receptor, where LH and testosterone fall. Flutamide is an androgen receptor blocker that can be given concurrently to nullify the transiently increased androgen effects induced by the former agents. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Drug receptors) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 68-year-old man is admitted to the hospital for management of a complicated femoral fracture. On hospital day 5, he develops fever, productive cough, and shortness of breath. Temperature is 38.5°C (101.3°F), blood pressure is 128/76 mm Hg, pulse is 98/min, respiratory rate is 20/min, and oxygen saturation is 94% on room air. Chest auscultation reveals crackles in the right lower lung field. Chest xray shows a new right lower lobe infiltrate, and sputum culture grows Pseudomonas aeruginosa. The patient is started on a combination of antibiotics. Prior to therapy, his serum creatinine was 0.9 mg/dL; on day 7 of treatment, it rises to 2.1 mg/dL. Serum potassium is 5.2 mEq/L. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for these findings?
explanation:
Aminoglycosides such as gentamicin, tobramycin, and amikacin cause acute tubular necrosis and ototoxicity. The high creatinine and potassium in this patient reflect acute renal failure.
Aminoglycosides inhibit the 30S ribosomal subunit and are effective against aerobic gram-negative rods.
Other HY agents that cause nephrotoxicity are cisplatin (a chemotherapeutic agent) and IV contrast (contrast nephropathy).
bottom_line: Aminoglycosides cause ATN and ototoxicity. They inhibit the 30S ribosomal subunit and target gram-negative rods. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 39-year-old man presents to the emergency department due to persistent penile discomfort that began earlier in the day. He reports an erection that has not resolved for the past six hours despite rest and a cold shower. He denies any recent sexual activity, trauma, or use of recreational drugs. He recalls starting a new medication recently for difficulty initiating and maintaining erections, which had been a source of frustration over the past year. He has no significant past medical history and takes no other medications. He is in a monogamous relationship and denies penile discharge, dysuria, or urinary frequency. Physical examination reveals a fully erect penis with a tense shaft and soft glans. There is tenderness to palpation of the corpora cavernosa, but no ecchymosis, ulceration, or urethral discharge. Vital signs are stable. What is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms?
explanation:
This patient presents with a persistent, painful erection following recent use of tadalafil, which strongly suggests a diagnosis of priapism—a urologic emergency defined as an erection lasting more than 4 hours. PDE-5 inhibitors such as sildenafil and tadalafil increase cGMP levels, resulting in prolonged smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation within the corpora cavernosa. In some individuals, this mechanism can lead to
unregulated blood trapping in the penis, failing to subside after sexual stimulation ceases, resulting in ischemic priapism.
PDE-5 inhibitors are commonly used in the treatment of erectile dysfunction and pulmonary hypertension. They inhibit the breakdown of cGMP by phosphodiesterase-5, allowing for prolonged vasodilation and erection maintenance. However, in rare cases, especially when combined with other vasoactive substances or predisposing conditions like sickle cell disease, they can provoke prolonged erections due to impaired venous
outflow.
Management of ischemic priapism includes initial decompression through aspiration of the corpora cavernosa, followed by intracavernosal injection of alpha-1 adrenergic agonists like phenylephrine to induce vasoconstriction and reduce penile blood pooling. Prompt intervention is critical to prevent long-term complications such as erectile dysfunction and tissue necrosis.
bottom_line: Priapism is a prolonged, often painful erection lasting more than 4 hours and is a urologic emergency. One of the most common pharmacologic causes is the use of phosphodiesterase-5 (PDE-5) inhibitors, such as tadalafil, which increase cGMP-mediated vasodilation in the corpus cavernosum. Prompt recognition and treatment, often with alpha-adrenergic agonists like phenylephrine, are critical to prevent permanent erectile tissue damage. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 72-year-old man comes to the clinic for follow-up of hypertension. His blood pressure was poorly controlled on hydrochlorothiazide alone, so amlodipine was added to his regimen 6 weeks ago. Since then, his systolic pressure has improved from 168 mm Hg to 132 mm Hg, and he reports generally feeling better. However, he has developed painless bilateral ankle swelling that worsens by the end of the day and improves slightly overnight. He denies dyspnea, chest pain, orthopnea, or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. There is no history of deep vein thrombosis or liver disease. His medications include amlodipine, hydrochlorothiazide, and aspirin. He does not use NSAIDs or steroids. On exam, vital signs are normal, and there is 2+ pitting edema bilaterally in the lower extremities. Jugular venous pressure is normal, and heart and lung exams are unremarkable. There is no hepatomegaly or ascites. Labs including renal function, albumin, and BNP are all within normal limits. ECG shows normal sinus rhythm. Echocardiogram from 3 months ago showed normal left ventricular function. The clinician suspects the edema is related to the patient's antihypertensive medication. Which of the following best explains this patient’s peripheral edema?

options.B: Decreased cardiac output due to negative inotropic effect
options.C: Impaired lymphatic drainage due to venous compression
options.D: Preferential dilation of precapillary arterioles over postcapillary venules
options.E: Reduced plasma oncotic pressure from albumin displacement

answer_text: Preferential dilation of precapillary arterioles over postcapillary venules
explanation:
Amlodipine is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that lowers blood pressure through vasodilation of vascular smooth muscle, primarily in the systemic arterioles. One of its most common side effects is peripheral edema, occurring in up to 10–20% of patients. This is not due to fluid overload or heart failure but rather a hemodynamic imbalance at the capillary level. Specifically, amlodipine causes preferential dilation of
precapillary arterioles, which increases hydrostatic pressure in capillary beds. Because the postcapillary venules remain relatively constricted, this pressure difference leads to fluid transudation into the interstitial space, especially in dependent areas like the lower extremities.
This side effect is dose-dependent and tends to develop within weeks of initiation. The edema is non-inflammatory, typically symmetric, and improves with leg elevation. Importantly, it does not reflect cardiac dysfunction, so diuretic therapy is often ineffective.
Understanding this mechanism helps distinguish drug-induced edema from edema caused by systemic conditions like heart failure, nephrotic syndrome, or hepatic disease. Unlike these other etiologies, amlodipine-induced edema typically lacks other findings like elevated JVP, pulmonary rales, or hypoalbuminemia. Recognition of the mechanism is key to avoiding unnecessary investigations or diuretic overuse. The best
solution is often to reduce the dose or switch to another class. Patient reassurance is also important, as the edema is benign and reversible.
bottom_line: Dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers like amlodipine or nifedipine cause peripheral edema by preferentially dilating precapillary arterioles, increasing capillary hydrostatic pressure and fluid leakage into the interstitium. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain, fatigue, and low-grade fevers for the past 3 weeks. He reports symmetric stiffness in his wrists, elbows, and knees, especially in the mornings, which improves throughout the day. He also notes intermittent muscle aches and a photosensitive rash across his cheeks and nose that appeared after a recent fishing trip. He denies prior history of autoimmune disease. His past medical history is notable for hypertension and a history of ventricular arrhythmias, for which he has been taking procainamide for the past 5 months. He takes no other medications, does not use over-the-counter supplements, and has no known allergies. He is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical exam, he has mild swelling and tenderness of the wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. There is an erythematous maculopapular rash across the malar region that spares the nasolabial folds. Cardiopulmonary and neurologic exams are normal. Laboratory testing reveals a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) titer and mildly elevated ESR. Additional testing is ordered to evaluate the suspected autoimmune etiology. Which of the following serologic markers is most specific for this patient's condition?
explanation:
This patient’s presentation—constitutional symptoms, arthralgias, photosensitive rash, and a positive ANA in the context of recent procainamide use—is consistent with drug-induced lupus erythematosus (DILE). DILE is a lupus-like syndrome triggered by certain medications, most commonly hydralazine, procainamide, isoniazid, and minocycline. The clinical features of DILE overlap with idiopathic systemic lupus erythematosus
(SLE), but they tend to be milder, often limited to arthralgias, myalgias, and skin findings without significant renal or central nervous system involvement.
The hallmark serologic finding in DILE is the presence of anti-histone antibodies, which are present in over 95% of cases. These antibodies target histone proteins involved in chromatin structure and are highly sensitive for drug-induced lupus, though not entirely specific.
While ANA is almost always positive in both DILE and SLE, the detection of anti-histone antibodies in the setting of a known offending drug is the most useful diagnostic clue for DILE. Importantly, unlike idiopathic SLE, anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith antibodies are rarely positive in DILE, and hypocomplementemia is uncommon.
The mainstay of treatment for drug-induced lupus is discontinuation of the offending agent, which typically leads to symptom resolution over weeks. Immunosuppressive therapy is rarely required, although NSAIDs may be used for symptomatic relief of arthralgias. Recognizing DILE is critical to avoid unnecessary immunosuppressive treatment and to guide appropriate discontinuation of the culprit drug.
bottom_line: Drug-induced lupus erythematosus is commonly associated with medications like procainamide and is strongly linked to the presence of anti-histone antibodies. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
topic: Steroids can cause myopathy. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System
stem: A 42-year-old man presents to clinic with worsening difficulty climbing stairs and rising from a chair over the past month. He has a 4-year history of autoimmune connective tissue disease that was initially controlled with high-dose prednisone. He reports that when first diagnosed, he had significant weakness that improved rapidly after starting steroids. Over the past year, however, his face has become rounder, he has developed easy bruising, and he notices fat deposition on the back of his neck. He has not had fever, rash, or joint pain recently. He denies numbness, tingling, or loss of bowel or bladder control. He has no weight loss, diarrhea, or cold intolerance. He does not drink alcohol and has never used illicit drugs. On examination, the patient is alert and oriented. His vital signs are normal. He has a rounded “moon” facies, thin extremities, and truncal obesity. Muscle strength testing reveals 4/5 strength in the hip flexors and shoulder abductors, while distal muscles of the hands and feet are normal. Reflexes are intact, and sensation is preserved in all extremities. The abdomen is soft and nontender. No rashes, nodules, or joint swelling are noted. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Chronic glucocorticoid use can lead to an insidious onset of proximal muscle weakness without associated pain. This is distinct from inflammatory myopathies, which typically produce both weakness and elevated muscle enzymes due to ongoing muscle inflammation and necrosis. Corticosteroid myopathy is characterized by type II muscle fiber atrophy, resulting from the catabolic effects of glucocorticoids on muscle protein.
Clinically, patients present with difficulty climbing stairs, standing from a seated position, or lifting objects overhead, all while sensation and reflexes remain intact.
Another important distinguishing factor is that corticosteroid myopathy typically occurs in patients who have signs of Cushingoid features from long-term steroid exposure, such as moon facies, truncal obesity, and easy bruising. Unlike a disease flare, muscle enzymes are usually normal, because the weakness arises from muscle atrophy rather than inflammation. A biopsy would show atrophy without evidence of inflammatory
infiltrates, separating it further from dermatomyositis or polymyositis. Recognition of corticosteroid myopathy is essential to avoid mistaking it for recurrence of the autoimmune disease, since increasing steroids would only worsen the condition.
Treatment of corticosteroid myopathy involves lowering the steroid dose if possible and considering steroid-sparing agents such as methotrexate, azathioprine, or mycophenolate. Physical therapy can also help improve muscle strength and function. The prognosis is generally good if steroids are tapered and alternate therapy is used, though recovery may take weeks to months. This patient’s proximal weakness, preserved
reflexes and sensation, and physical exam features of steroid excess strongly support corticosteroid myopathy as the diagnosis.
bottom_line: Chronic glucocorticoid use can lead to an insidious onset of proximal muscle weakness without associated pain.

subject: Pharmacology
topic: Amiodarone causes pulmonary fibrosis. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System
stem: A 62-year-old woman presents with gradually worsening shortness of breath. She notices it most when she climbs the small hill behind her house. She describes a dry, persistent cough that interrupts conversations. The cough began months ago and has become more frequent. She denies wheezing. She denies chest pain at rest. She feels winded when carrying groceries from the car. She has started taking shorter walks in the evening. She sleeps with an extra pillow for comfort. She has not had fevers. She has not had night sweats. She has noticed mild, unintentional weight loss. She has not traveled recently. She has no known occupational dust exposure. She does not keep birds. She has never worked in mines. She has never sandblasted or cut stone. She does not smoke. She drinks wine socially on weekends. She has a history of atrial fibrillation. Her arrhythmia was diagnosed two years ago. She was started on amiodarone after several emergency visits. The medication allowed her to maintain normal rhythm for long stretches. She recalls being told to avoid grapefruit. She also recalls being told to protect her skin from the sun. She has noticed a faint blue-gray tint on sun-exposed areas. She sometimes sees halos around lights at night. Her energy has slowly declined. She denies leg swelling. She denies pleuritic chest pain. She denies joint pain. She denies rash aside from photosensitivity. She has no pets at home. She teaches a community class on weather and climate. Meteorology is her favorite hobby. She keeps a cloud journal on her porch. Cumulus clouds make her think of scattered thoughts that build through the day. Cumulonimbus towers remind her of summer drama. Stratus decks feel to her like a blanket that quiets the town. She watches cirrus filaments for hints of change. She listens for shifts in wind from sea breeze to land breeze. She teaches students how fronts shape the sky. El Niño years energize her conversations. La Niña years sharpen her forecasts. She distinguishes maritime tropical air from continental polar air with ease. She sketches jet stream meanders on napkins. She compares trade winds to steady routines. She calls gust fronts the breath of summer storms. She times the arrival of a cold front by smell and feel. Her neighbors ask her when to cover their gardens. She tracks barometer trends like plot twists. She photographs KelvinHelmholtz waves when rare. She points out mammatus beneath anvils after sunset. She notes fallstreak holes with quiet delight. She follows Saharan dust plumes across the ocean on maps. She explains atmospheric rivers to curious children. She teaches that maritime polar air feels raw and clean. She contrasts continental tropical air as heavy and hazy. She says weather is a story the sky tells. Lately the story feels short of breath. She pauses halfway up her driveway. She stops to breathe after making the bed. She coughs with laughter. She coughs when reading aloud. She misses finishing sentences without a breath. She has tried over-the-counter cough remedies. They did not help. She once had pneumonia as a teenager. She recovered fully. She had no lung problems for decades. Her father had emphysema from heavy smoking. Her mother has seasonal allergies. She has never had asthma. She has never had tuberculosis. She had a chest image a year ago for palpitations. It was unremarkable. She has been taking the same amiodarone daily since diagnosis. She learned it can affect the thyroid. She once felt unusually cold in spring. Her skin became drier. She attributed that to aging. She also noticed her hair texture change. She did not seek evaluation at that time. Her cardiologist praised her rhythm control. Her pulse felt steady at most visits. She appreciated sleeping without fluttering sensations. Over the past six months, breathing has become her main worry. She says talking about clouds now makes her cough. She cancelled a planned lecture on frontal dynamics last week. Walking across a breezy parking lot leaves her winded. She feels a tight band across the lower chest when she exerts. It is not sharp. It is not positional. It eases with rest. She denies swelling in her ankles. She denies heartburn. She denies postnasal drip. She denies hoarseness. She has not changed detergents or soaps. She has not started new medications except occasional acetaminophen. She has no recent travel to high elevations. She still enjoys watching cold air damming events. She notes that inversions have been frequent this winter. She says smoke from distant fires drifted in last summer. Her symptoms began before that season. Spirometry shows a restrictive lung pattern. Chest xray shows reticulonodularity with honeycombing. Which of the following is the most likely adverse effect of this patient's amiodarone?
explanation:
Amiodarone is a potent antiarrhythmic that can cause pulmonary fibrosis as a long-term complication. The drug accumulates in lung tissue and induces direct cytotoxicity, phospholipid accumulation, and inflammatory injury, ultimately leading to scarring of the interstitium. Patients typically present with slowly progressive dyspnea and dry cough. Spirometry in this condition often reveals a restrictive pattern characterized by a
normal or increased FEV1/FVC ratio due to proportionally reduced FEV1 and FVC. Chest x-ray and high-resolution CT commonly show reticulonodular opacities, interstitial thickening, and honeycombing, particularly in the lung bases, reflecting the fibrotic remodeling of lung parenchyma.
Beyond pulmonary toxicity, amiodarone has systemic effects due to its iodine content and tissue accumulation. The thyroid gland is especially susceptible, and patients may develop hypothyroidism (more common) or hyperthyroidism (less common). Cutaneous manifestations include a blue-gray discoloration of the skin, especially on sun-exposed areas, due to dermal drug deposition and photosensitivity. Together with
pulmonary fibrosis, thyroid dysfunction, and cutaneous changes, these adverse effects highlight the need for careful monitoring of patients on chronic amiodarone therapy with both pulmonary function assessment and regular systemic evaluation.
bottom_line: Amiodarone is a potent antiarrhythmic that can cause pulmonary fibrosis as a long-term complication.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic for follow-up of primary infertility. She has been receiving pharmacologic ovulation induction for the past 3 months. Her menstrual cycles were previously irregular, but she has recently achieved consistent ovulation confirmed by mid-luteal progesterone testing. She feels well today and denies abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, fever, or urinary symptoms. Physical examination is unremarkable, and laboratory studies are within normal limits. A pelvic ultrasound performed as part of routine monitoring shows bilateral enlarged ovaries with multiple developing follicles. Which of the following complications is this patient at greatest risk of developing due to her current therapy?
explanation:
Ovulatory induction enlarges the ovaries by recruiting multiple growing follicles, creating heavy, cystic adnexa that are more mobile within the pelvis. The ovary can rotate around the infundibulopelvic (suspensory) ligament—which carries the ovarian vessels—and the utero-ovarian ligament. Venous and lymphatic outflow are compromised first, causing stromal edema and further enlargement that tightens the twist; with time,
arterial inflow is reduced, leading to ischemia and possible necrosis. This mechanical vulnerability is the core reason ovulatory induction increases the risk of ovarian torsion, even when patients are asymptomatic at a monitoring visit.
Clinical presentation, when it occurs, is abrupt unilateral pelvic or lower abdominal pain often with nausea and vomiting; pain may wax and wane if intermittent detorsion occurs. Exam can reveal adnexal tenderness or fullness; fever and leukocytosis are late and variable. Risk factors include pharmacologic ovulation induction or assisted reproduction, pregnancy (enlarged corpus luteum and increased laxity), preexisting ovarian
cysts or masses (≥5–6 cm), prior torsion, and elongated or lax adnexal ligaments. Related but distinct therapy complications—such as ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome—can coexist and further enlarge ovaries, amplifying torsion risk.
Diagnosis is clinical with urgent confirmation by transvaginal ultrasonography with Doppler. Typical findings include an enlarged edematous ovary, peripheralized follicles, stromal edema, free pelvic fluid, and reduced or absent venous (early) or arterial (later) flow; the “whirlpool sign” may be seen in the twisted pedicle. Importantly, the presence of some Doppler flow does not exclude torsion due to dual blood supply from the
ovarian and uterine arteries, so imaging is interpreted in the context of risk and symptoms.
Management is prompt gynecologic intervention—preferably laparoscopic detorsion with ovarian preservation whenever viable, often combined with cystectomy if a benign cyst is the lead point. Oophoropexy may be considered for recurrent torsion or high-risk anatomy. While a patient may be asymptomatic during routine monitoring, counseling is essential: strict return precautions for sudden pelvic pain, avoidance of vigorous
activity during stimulation cycles, and careful dose/follicle monitoring to limit extreme ovarian enlargement.
bottom_line: Ovulatory induction enlarges the ovaries, predisposing them to torsion—a surgical emergency—whereas appendicitis, diverticulitis, PID, and ureterolithiasis are unrelated processes. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY Adverse effects) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 29-year-old man is diagnosed with cluster headache after presenting with severe right-sided periorbital pain that consistently awakens him from sleep. The pain lasts approximately 30 minutes and is associated with ipsilateral lacrimation and nasal congestion. He reports pacing around the room during episodes due to the intensity of the pain. Neurologic examination is normal. He was successfully treated in the ED with 100% oxygen via nonrebreather mask and discharged. He returns now asking how to prevent future episodes. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
explanation:
The most appropriate next step in management for this patient is initiating verapamil, the first-line prophylactic agent for cluster headache. Verapamil is a non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker that acts on nodal calcium channels, making it effective not only in this setting but also in atrial fibrillation for rate control.
In cluster headache, it is considered the mainstay of long-term prevention due to its ability to modulate hypothalamic and trigeminal-autonomic pathways involved in the disease mechanism. Prophylaxis should be started after an acute episode is treated — ideally during the same cluster cycle — to reduce the frequency, duration, and severity of attacks.
Cluster headaches are characterized by recurrent, unilateral, severe orbital or periorbital pain, often occurring during sleep and accompanied by ipsilateral lacrimation, rhinorrhea, ptosis, or miosis. Patients with cluster headaches may report pacing during attacks due to the severity of the pain. These attacks last minutes to half hour and tend to cluster for weeks with remission periods in between.
Abortive therapy (i.e., oxygen) treats the acute episode, but verapamil is essential to prevent recurrence. A HY side-effect of verapamil on USMLE is constipation.
bottom_line: Cluster headache presents with severe, unilateral periorbital pain that wakes patients from sleep and is associated with ipsilateral lacrimation and nasal congestion. On USMLE, acute treatment is 100% oxygen, while verapamil is the drug of choice for prophylaxis. The HY side-effect of verapamil is constipation. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 28-year-old woman comes to the physician because of a 6-month history of progressive shortness of breath and dry cough. She also reports feeling increasing fatigue and occasional chest discomfort. Her medical history includes a recent diagnosis of rheumatoid arthritis, for which she is on methotrexate. On physical examination, she has bilateral crackles in the lower lung fields and pitting edema in both legs. Pulmonary function tests show reduced FVC with a relatively preserved FEV1. A high-resolution CT scan of the chest shows ground-glass opacities and reticulations. Which of the following is most likely to be seen in this patient?

options.A: Charcot-Leyden eosinophilic crystalline infiltration B. Diffuse interstitial fibrosis with lymphocytic infiltration C. Necrotizing granulomas with cavitary lesions and caseous necrosis D. Noncaseating granulomas and focal interstitial infiltrates with upper lung predominance E. Panacinar emphysema with air trapping and inflammation of the alveolar spaces

explanation:
The patient has rheumatoid arthritis, and you need to know that 1) methotrexate, the first-line disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drug (DMARD) can cause pulmonary fibrosis, and 2) RA - the autoimmune disease itself - can directly increase the risk of pulmonary fibrosis, which is called "rheumatoid lung." So when patients with RA get pulmonary fibrosis, you say, "Is it the methotrexate or the RA?" The answer is both.
Fibrosis causes a restrictive lung pattern of decreased lung volumes and a normal/increased FEV1/FVC. The words "reticular," "reticulonodular," and "reticulations" on chest x-ray or CT all mean honeycombing and are highly buzzy for fibrosis. "Reticulogranular" is an exception that can be seen in neonatal respiratory distress syndrome Qs as per my observation, but this is clearly a disparate/unrelated presentation and
demographic. But for everyone who's not an NRDS presentation, these terms mean pulmonary fibrosis / honeycombing for USMLE.
Lymphocytic infiltrates are often seen in patients with pulmonary fibrosis, which explains why prednisone (a steroid) can have utility in the treatment. Another drug that shows up on USMLE for pulmonary fibrosis is pirfenidone, which is an inhibitor of TBF-beta-induced pulmonary fibrosis.
bottom_line: Rheumatoid arthritis, and the first-line DMARD to treat it, methotrexate, both cause pulmonary fibrosis. Fibrosis causes a restrictive lung pattern on spirometry and will be described on CXR and CT as reticular or reticulonodular, which mean honeycombing. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) Syste

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 76-year-old man comes to the clinic for routine follow-up. He was hospitalized 3 weeks ago for palpitations and shortness of breath and was found to have an irregularly irregular pulse. At that time, he was started on metoprolol and discharged in stable condition. His medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus and coronary artery disease. He reports no ongoing symptoms and says his energy has improved. Current medications include aspirin, metoprolol, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. Vital signs are normal. ECG shows continued atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 80/min. Which of the following is the most appropriate step for ongoing longitudinal management?

options.D: Refer for left atrial appendage occlusion

explanation:
This patient has atrial fibrillation (AF) with a CHA₂DS₂-VASc score of at least 4 (age >75 = 2, diabetes = 1, coronary artery disease = 1), placing him at high risk for thromboembolic events. Stroke prevention is the cornerstone of long-term AF management, and anticoagulation is indicated regardless of whether the AF is paroxysmal or persistent. Dabigatran is a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) approved for stroke prevention in
nonvalvular AF and is an appropriate first-line choice. It does not require routine INR monitoring and has a relatively favorable safety profile compared to warfarin, especially with regard to intracranial hemorrhage. It should be noted, however, that warfarin can still acceptably be used.
Long-term management of AF follows two pathways: rate/rhythm control and stroke prevention. While rate control has already been initiated with metoprolol and rhythm control may be considered if symptoms persist, anticoagulation is non-negotiable when the stroke risk is high. This patient’s age and comorbidities make him an ideal candidate for a DOAC. The question tests whether the examinee can recognize the importance
of stroke prevention even in a clinically stable, asymptomatic AF patient. Dabigatran, along with other DOACs such as apixaban and rivaroxaban, can be used as an alternative to warfarin in most patients with nonvalvular AF.
bottom_line: Long-term management of atrial fibrillation hinges on stroke prevention in high-risk patients, which requires oral anticoagulation. In stable patients, DOACs (like dabigatran) or warfarin are the preferred first-line agents for reducing thromboembolic risk. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 29-year-old man comes to the clinic for follow-up due to ongoing respiratory symptoms. He reports having asthma since childhood, which he has managed solely with an albuterol inhaler that he uses “a few times a week,” mostly when he’s exercising or exposed to cold air. Over the past two months, however, he has noticed that he’s using his inhaler almost daily and is waking up 1–2 nights per week with coughing and wheezing. He denies recent infections or hospitalizations but admits that he hasn’t seen a doctor in over a year. He works as a warehouse stocker and is often exposed to dust and fumes. He has no history of smoking, and his BMI is 24. Physical examination reveals mild expiratory wheezing but is otherwise normal. His peak expiratory flow is 80% of predicted. Pulmonary function testing done 6 months ago showed reversible obstruction. The physician determines that the patient is not currently in an acute exacerbation and is well enough for outpatient management. However, his current symptoms suggest a change in asthma severity. What is the next best step in management?

options.A: Add inhaled fluticasone to his regimen
options.C: Increase frequency of albuterol as needed
options.E: Refer for chest CT to evaluate for occupational lung disease

answer_text: Add inhaled fluticasone to his regimen
explanation:
This patient has progressed from intermittent to mild persistent asthma, which is defined by symptoms more than twice per week, nighttime awakenings more than twice per month, and minor activity limitation. His increased use of albuterol and nighttime symptoms clearly meet this threshold. According to the National Asthma Education and Prevention Program (NAEPP) guidelines, the first-line treatment for mild persistent
asthma is the addition of a low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) such as fluticasone. ICSs are the most effective anti-inflammatory medications for long-term control and reduce the risk of exacerbations, emergency visits, and long-term airway remodeling.
Albuterol provides rapid bronchodilation, but it does not treat the underlying inflammation, which is the fundamental pathology in asthma. Continued reliance on albuterol alone is a sign of poor control and increases the risk of future severe exacerbations. Adding an ICS like fluticasone helps suppress airway inflammation, reduce hyperresponsiveness, and stabilize long-term symptoms. Patients should also receive education on
proper inhaler technique, be scheduled for follow-up to assess response, and be given a written asthma action plan. This stepwise approach ensures appropriate treatment escalation as the disease progresses.
Failing to escalate therapy here would miss a critical opportunity to control the disease early and improve quality of life. The addition of inhaled fluticasone is appropriate, evidence-based, and prevents future complications.
bottom_line: Patients with mild persistent asthma require step-up therapy with a daily inhaled corticosteroid. Fluticasone reduces airway inflammation, improves symptoms, and prevents future exacerbations better than albuterol alone. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 6-month-old girl born at 36 weeks' gestation is evaluated as part of her pediatric screening. Her mother reports a mucopurulent discharge from the nose. The teeth appear unusually shaped. Fundoscopic examination is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate pharmacologic therapy?
explanation:
The diagnosis is congenital syphilis. Four characteristic findings for USMLE are 1) the "snuffles," which presents as a nasal discharge, sometimes described as mucopurulent, usually within the first few months of life; 2) Hutchinson teeth, which have a characteristic mulberry shape; 3) saddle nose (more of a late finding); and 4) Saber shins (bowing of the tibias, which can resemble rickets).
Neurosensory hearing loss and cataracts can also be seen, but these aren't specific for syphilis and are also seen in congenital rubella.
USMLE wants you to know that syphilis (Treponema pallidum) is a spirochete, which is a spiral-shaped gram-negative bacterium.
Penicillin is the HY treatment. It blocks cell wall peptidoglycan synthesis by binding to penicillin-binding proteins such as transpeptidase. Even though beta-lactams are notably used against gram-positives, they are also effective against certain gram-negatives, like syphilis.
bottom_line: Congenital syphilis is caused by Treponema pallidum, a spirochete. Nasal discharge, teeth abnormalities, saddle nose, and Saber shins are HY findings. Treat with penicillin. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 56-year-old man is evaluated in the cardiology clinic for recurrent episodes of substernal chest discomfort. He describes the pain as tight and pressure-like, occurring primarily at rest and sometimes waking him from sleep. He denies exertional angina and reports that exercise tolerance has not significantly changed. His history is significant for long-standing hypertension and hyperlipidemia, but he has no history of diabetes or smoking. Medications include a statin and a thiazide diuretic. Physical examination today is unremarkable, and his resting ECG is normal. Stress testing shows no ischemic changes during exercise. To further evaluate his symptoms, a coronary angiography is performed with intracoronary acetylcholine infusion. Which of the following best describes the expected vascular response in this patient?
explanation:
In healthy endothelium, acetylcholine stimulates muscarinic M3 receptors on endothelial cells, causing nitric oxide release, smooth muscle relaxation, and vasodilation. However, in patients with coronary endothelial dysfunction or vasospastic angina (Prinzmetal angina), the endothelium cannot generate adequate nitric oxide. In this setting, acetylcholine acts directly on vascular smooth muscle muscarinic receptors, producing
vasoconstriction. The paradoxical constriction provoked during acetylcholine challenge testing confirms endothelial dysfunction and can reproduce the patient’s symptoms of chest discomfort at rest.
Clinically, acetylcholine or ergonovine testing is used to provoke coronary vasospasm in suspected vasospastic angina when angiography does not reveal fixed stenosis. Vasospasm causes transient myocardial ischemia, ST-segment elevation during attacks, and chest pain typically at rest or during the night. Unlike stable angina, symptoms are not precipitated by exertion but by transient focal coronary constriction.
Management of vasospastic angina focuses on vasodilators such as calcium channel blockers and nitrates. Beta-blockers, particularly nonselective ones, are avoided because they may exacerbate spasm by allowing unopposed alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction. Identifying endothelial dysfunction is important not only in variant angina but also in early atherosclerosis, as impaired vasodilatory responses precede overt plaque
formation.
bottom_line: Intracoronary acetylcholine normally triggers vasodilation via nitric oxide release, but in patients with endothelial dysfunction or vasospastic angina it paradoxically causes vasoconstriction. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for this patient’s condition?

options.A: Chronic oral prednisone therapy for long-term suppression
options.C: Oral antihistamines as sole therapy for inflammation control
options.D: Topical corticosteroids and avoidance of triggers

answer_text: Topical corticosteroids and avoidance of triggers
explanation:
This patient’s chronic, intensely pruritic, ill-defined eczematous plaques on the flexural elbows with excoriations are classic for atopic dermatitis. The image provided supports lichenified erythematous patches without purulence or sharply demarcated borders. The relapsing course with seasonal worsening and partial response to emollients further aligns with a diagnosis of atopic dermatitis rather than infection, psoriasis, or
contact dermatitis. Initial management targets inflammation control and barrier repair: short courses of low- to medium-potency topical corticosteroids to active lesions plus avoidance of known triggers.
Pathobiology centers on epidermal barrier dysfunction and Th2-skewed inflammation. Loss-of-function variants in filaggrin impair stratum corneum integrity, increasing transepidermal water loss and allergen penetration. Keratinocytes and type 2 cytokines (IL-4, IL-13) amplify itch and inflammation, creating an itch–scratch cycle that drives lichenification. Colonization with skin bacteria is common but not synonymous with
infection; routine antibiotics are unnecessary in the absence of impetiginization.
Risk factors include personal or family atopy, early-life xerosis, low humidity, frequent hot bathing, harsh soaps or fragrances, abrasive fabrics (e.g., wool), sweating, stress, and secondary irritant exposures. Patients often follow the “atopic march,” with later allergic rhinitis or asthma. Complications include sleep disturbance, excoriation and lichen simplex chronicus, secondary bacterial infection (impetigo), eczema herpeticum
with widespread HSV during flares, and post-inflammatory dyspigmentation; prolonged or inappropriate systemic steroid use adds iatrogenic risk.
Management begins with daily liberal emollients, gentle skin care (lukewarm baths, non-soap cleansers), avoidance of triggers, and topical corticosteroids to inflamed areas with potency tailored to site and severity (low potency for thin skin, medium for thicker flexural sites for brief courses). Topical calcineurin inhibitors are steroid-sparing options for sensitive areas or maintenance; phototherapy or targeted biologic therapy (e.g.,
IL-4Rα blockade) is reserved for moderate–severe refractory disease. Antihistamines may help sleep but do not treat the dermatitis. Prevention focuses on barrier-first care: regular thick moisturizers after bathing, trigger identification, short nails, soft cotton clothing, and consideration of dilute bleach baths only for patients with recurrent bacterial superinfection.
bottom_line: Atopic dermatitis presents with itchy flexural rashes from barrier dysfunction and Th2 inflammation. Initial treatment is emollients, trigger avoidance, and topical corticosteroids. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 33-year-old woman presents with complaints of fatigue, palpitations, and unintentional weight loss over the past two months. She reports increased sweating, heat intolerance, and occasional tremors. Her menstrual cycles have become irregular, and she has difficulty sleeping at night. She denies recent illness, medication changes, or illicit drug use. She has no significant past medical history, and there is no family history of autoimmune disease. On physical examination, she appears anxious with fine hand tremors. Her heart rate is 118/min and blood pressure is 138/72 mmHg. Her thyroid gland is diffusely enlarged and non-tender. Laboratory studies show decreased thyroid-stimulating hormone and elevated thyroxine. She is started on a medication as part of her initial management. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of this drug?

options.A: Blockade of iodination and coupling reactions in thyroid hormone synthesis
options.B: Destruction of thyroid follicular cells via radioactive uptake
options.C: Inhibition of sodium-iodide symporter at the basolateral thyroid membrane
options.D: Inhibition of thyroid hormone release from thyroid follicles
options.E: Replacement of deficient thyroid hormone with exogenous analogue

answer_text: Blockade of iodination and coupling reactions in thyroid hormone synthesis
explanation:
This patient’s tremor, heat intolerance, weight loss, tachycardia, and diffuse, nontender goiter with suppressed TSH and elevated thyroxine indicate thyrotoxicosis, most consistent with Graves disease. Initial antithyroid therapy commonly uses a thionamide; methimazole acts by preventing new thyroid hormone formation inside the follicle rather than by blocking release of preformed hormone. Clinically, this means symptom
relief is supported up front with a non-selective β-blocker while methimazole reduces synthesis over days to weeks, after which dosing is titrated by periodic free T4/TSH monitoring to avoid iatrogenic hypothyroidism.
Methimazole’s molecular target is thyroid peroxidase, the luminal enzyme that performs three key steps in hormone biosynthesis: iodide oxidation, iodination (organification) of tyrosyl residues on thyroglobulin, and coupling of iodotyrosines to form iodothyronines. By blocking organification and coupling, methimazole prevents formation of monoiodotyrosine and diiodotyrosine and stops their coupling into T3 and T4, effectively
shutting down new hormone synthesis. Propylthiouracil shares this intrathyroidal effect and uniquely also inhibits peripheral 5′-deiodination of T4 to T3, which is why PTU is preferred in thyroid storm and during the first trimester of pregnancy, whereas methimazole is generally preferred otherwise due to better safety with long-term use.
Diagnosis of hyperthyroidism rests on low TSH with elevated free T4 and/or T3, with etiologic clarification by radioactive iodine uptake: diffuse uptake in Graves, focal/patchy uptake in toxic nodular disease, and low uptake in thyroiditis or exogenous hormone use. Management options include thionamides (to induce euthyroidism), radioactive iodine ablation (definitive therapy in many nonpregnant adults), or thyroidectomy
(preferred for very large goiters, coexisting ophthalmopathy in selected cases, or when rapid control is needed and RAI is unsuitable). Symptom control uses non-selective β-blockers; iodine solutions can transiently inhibit hormone release (Wolff–Chaikoff effect) in specific preoperative or storm settings; cholestyramine can augment clearance in severe disease.
Adverse effects of thionamides include rash, arthralgias, cholestasis (more with methimazole), hepatotoxicity (notably with PTU), and rare agranulocytosis—any fever or sore throat warrants immediate drug cessation and urgent CBC. Methimazole is teratogenic in early pregnancy (e.g., aplasia cutis, esophageal/choanal atresia), so PTU is used in the first trimester with a switch to methimazole thereafter; both drugs can cross
the placenta and into breast milk, so the lowest effective dose is used with close monitoring.
bottom_line: Methimazole acts at the molecular level by binding to thyroid peroxidase in the follicular lumen, preventing oxidation of iodide to iodine and blocking the organification and coupling of tyrosyl residues on thyroglobulin. This halts formation of T3 and T4. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (HY use-cases) System

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 54-year-old man comes to the clinic for follow-up after starting a new blood pressure medication. One month ago, his blood pressure was 196/114 mm Hg during an annual physical, and he was started on lisinopril. At that time, his serum creatinine was 0.9 mg/dL, and serum electrolytes were normal. Today, he feels well and has no complaints. His blood pressure is 172/92 mm Hg. Physical examination shows no bruits, edema, or rash. He does not use NSAIDs or herbal supplements. Repeat labs reveal a serum creatinine of 2.6 mg/dL and no proteinuria or hematuria. Which of the following best explains the mechanism leading to his current laboratory findings?

options.A: Decreased sodium reabsorption in the distal nephron
options.B: Endothelial inflammation causing fibrinoid necrosis of renal arterioles
options.C: Impaired glomerular perfusion from loss of angiotensin II–mediated efferent vasoconstriction
options.D: Mesangial immune complex deposition leading to glomerular inflammation
options.E: Sympathetic overstimulation causing afferent arteriolar hypertrophy

answer_text: Impaired glomerular perfusion from loss of angiotensin II–mediated efferent vasoconstriction
explanation:
This patient’s rise in serum creatinine after initiation of an ACE inhibitor is due to a hemodynamic reduction in glomerular filtration pressure, driven by the loss of angiotensin II–mediated efferent arteriolar vasoconstriction. In renal artery stenosis, perfusion pressure distal to the stenosis is reduced, and the kidney compensates by increasing renin secretion, promoting angiotensin II production to constrict the efferent arteriole and
maintain intraglomerular pressure. When an ACE inhibitor is introduced, this compensatory mechanism is lost, and the resultant fall in glomerular hydrostatic pressure leads to a drop in GFR and a rise in creatinine. This effect is particularly pronounced in bilateral renal artery stenosis or solitary kidney scenarios, where perfusion is already critically dependent on angiotensin II. Importantly, this is a functional, reversible insult
rather than structural nephron damage.
In most patients, a mild (up to 30%) increase in creatinine is expected and acceptable when starting ACE inhibitors. However, a sudden, marked rise suggests renovascular compromise, prompting suspicion for bilateral renal artery stenosis. These patients often have diffuse atherosclerosis, abdominal bruits, or flash pulmonary edema. Diagnosis is confirmed with imaging like Doppler ultrasound, CT angiography, or MR
angiography. ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in bilateral disease due to risk of acute kidney injury but can be used in unilateral disease with careful monitoring if the contralateral kidney is healthy.
bottom_line: In patients with bilateral renal artery stenosis, ACE inhibitors can cause a sharp decline in GFR by disrupting angiotensin II–dependent efferent arteriolar constriction. The creatinine rise is functional and reversible, highlighting the importance of recognizing hemodynamic mechanisms in acute kidney injury. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System

subject: Pharmacology
topic: Doxorubicin causes dilated cardiomyopathy. Pharmacology Subject Pharmacology (General Principles) (Mechanisms of action) System
stem: A 66-year-old man comes to the clinic for a routine follow-up. He completed treatment for stage IIA Hodgkin lymphoma 3 years ago and has been in remission since.
